turns.content: What does PER actually measure and why do some analysts think it's flawed?

PER (Player Efficiency Rating) is John Hollinger’s attempt to compress a player’s per-minute box score production into a single number, normalized so league average is 15.0 each season. Conceptually it’s “box-score productivity per minute,” adjusted for pace, with positive weights for things like made shots, free throws, rebounds, assists, steals, blocks, and negative weights for misses and turnovers. At a high level, PER does three main things: 1) Builds an “unadjusted” efficiency formula from box score events with fixed coefficients (e.g., turnovers hurt, rebounds help). 2) Adjusts for team pace so players on faster teams aren’t automatically inflated. 3) Normalizes to a league average baseline. Why it’s criticized: - It’s box-score only, so it can’t see defense beyond steals/blocks and can’t capture spacing, screening, off-ball gravity, or scheme. - The coefficients bake in value judgments that can overvalue certain stats (notably defensive rebounds and blocks) and undervalue others (like on-ball defense or “hockey assists”). - It’s historically biased toward high-usage scorers because volume scoring (especially if reasonably efficient) tends to dominate the formula. - It conflates role and impact: a player can post a big PER in a small, favorable role without being as valuable as an all-around player in a tougher role. - It’s not context-aware: teammate quality, lineup context, and opponent quality are absent. If you want, I can walk through the structure of the PER formula and then contrast it with metrics like Win Shares, BPM, RAPM, and EPM so you can see what each is trying to measure.
Yeah, break down the PER formula structure. I don’t need every coefficient memorized, but I want to understand what’s inside it and where the bias comes from.

PER starts from an “unadjusted” per-minute efficiency calculation. The building blocks are: - Scoring: field goals made, three-point makes (implicitly via FG and 3P), free throws made, and a penalty for missed shots. - Playmaking: assists (positive) and turnovers (negative). - Rebounding: offensive and defensive rebounds (positive, with different weights). - Defense: steals and blocks (positive), personal fouls (negative), and sometimes a minor adjustment for team defensive performance. - Team context adjustments: pace adjustment and “team assist factor” components. The bias sources are mostly in (a) what it can observe and (b) the weights. 1) What it observes: PER sees: - Points and efficiency (through FG, FGA, FT, FTA). - “Countable” defensive events (STL, BLK). - Rebounds. PER does not see: - Shot quality created (beyond the assist count). - Defensive positioning, contest quality, deterrence, switching, communication. - Off-ball offense: screening, cutting, spacing value, gravity. - Passing that doesn’t become an assist, secondary assists, keeping the advantage. - Turnover context (some turnovers are “good” risks, some are sloppy; PER treats them similarly). 2) Weight choices: - Rebounds: Defensive rebounds are plentiful and partially team/system-driven, but they still get credit. This can inflate bigs who collect easy defensive boards. - Blocks: Blocks are rewarded, but blocks can be a noisy proxy for defense; some elite defenders block fewer shots but suppress attempts or force misses. - Assists: Assists get credit, but the assist statistic itself is dependent on teammates making shots and scorer’s table conventions, and it ignores passes that lead to fouls or advantage creation. - Usage/volume scoring: PER’s scoring component rewards points and efficiency. High-usage scorers with decent efficiency tend to do well, even if they have defensive shortcomings or the scoring comes with opportunity cost. 3) Pace adjustment: Pace adjustment tries to put everyone on an equal possession basis. But pace adjustment doesn’t remove all context; it just standardizes the “per minute” environment. A player on a fast team may still benefit indirectly from style effects (more transition, different shot mix). 4) Normalization: PER is normalized so league average is 15. This makes it easy to compare across seasons, but it can give a false sense of precision: a 21 PER vs 19 PER difference might not map cleanly to “this many more wins.” Bottom line: PER is best read as “box-score rate stat with pace adjustment,” not as a true impact metric. It can be a useful descriptive number for offensive box-score production, but it’s not a robust measure of two-way value.
So when people say PER “overvalues big men,” is that mostly the rebound and block weights? Or is there more to it?

Rebounds and blocks are a big part of it, but there are a few reinforcing mechanisms: 1) Rebounds are high-frequency and correlate with being tall/playing near the rim. Even if the weight per rebound isn’t enormous, the volume is. A center can rack up 10–12 rebounds nightly; a guard might get 3–5. That gap alone moves PER. 2) Blocks are rare but heavily “credited.” Blocks get a decent positive weight and are concentrated among rim protectors. Even if blocks aren’t perfectly correlated with defensive impact, they’re the kind of defensive event PER can “see,” so it treats them as valuable. 3) Shot selection and efficiency: Bigs often take a higher share of shots at the rim, which tend to be efficient. PER rewards efficient scoring. A center finishing at 65% on lower difficulty attempts can look extremely “efficient” by box score, even if the creation is provided by guards. PER doesn’t separate “created” vs “finished.” 4) Turnover and assist distributions: Guards handle the ball more, so they incur more turnovers. PER penalizes turnovers, and it doesn’t fully adjust for the fact that high-leverage creation roles naturally come with higher turnover rates. Assists help guards, but turnovers can counteract, especially for high-usage creators. 5) Minutes and role: Many bigs who play limited minutes can post gaudy per-minute stats (rebounding and rim finishing scale well per minute). PER is per minute, so a high-energy bench big can look like a star in PER without necessarily being able to handle starter-level minutes or responsibilities. So yes, rebound/block weights matter, but the deeper issue is that PER’s “observable universe” aligns better with traditional big-man box score contributions than with perimeter defensive value and advantage creation.
How does Win Shares differ conceptually? People talk like it’s “team wins allocated,” but I’m not sure what that means mechanically.

Win Shares (WS) is designed to allocate a team’s wins to individual players based on their estimated contributions to offense and defense, using a framework derived from Dean Oliver’s work. Mechanically, the idea is: 1) Estimate how many wins the team produced from its points scored and points allowed (via a Pythagorean expectation / marginal points-to-wins relationship). 2) Split those wins into “offensive wins” and “defensive wins” based on team offense/defense. 3) Allocate offensive wins to players based on offensive box-score contributions (shooting efficiency, usage, playmaking, offensive rebounds, etc.). 4) Allocate defensive wins to players based on defensive box-score contributions and team defensive performance while they’re on the floor—though in practice WS defense is heavily team-based and anchored to minutes and team defensive rating proxies. Key differences vs PER: - WS is cumulative, not rate-based (though WS/48 is the rate version). So minutes matter a lot. - WS is explicitly tied to team success; PER isn’t. - WS uses team context heavily, especially on defense. That can be good (acknowledges defense is team-connected) but also bad (it can misattribute team defense to individuals). “Allocated wins” means: given the team’s overall win total, WS assigns fractions of those wins to players. It’s not saying “this player independently created 7.2 wins in a vacuum,” but rather “based on this model and this team’s performance, the player is assigned 7.2 wins’ worth of credit.”
That sounds like it could be circular—if the team is good, everyone gets more win shares. Is that a fair critique?

Yes, that’s a common critique, and it’s partly inherent to the design. Because WS starts from team wins (or team point differential translated into wins), players on strong teams have more wins to “distribute.” Even if the allocation tries to apportion credit proportionally, the pool is larger. That means: - A solid starter on a 60-win team can accumulate more WS than a better player on a 35-win team, especially in cumulative WS. - Defensive WS, in particular, can reflect team defensive strength more than individual defensive impact. If you play heavy minutes on an elite defense, you’ll often accrue strong DWS even if your individual defense is merely okay. That said, proponents would argue: - If you’re evaluating “value in the context of actual wins,” tying to team success is a feature, not a bug. - For within-team comparisons, WS can be informative because the pool is shared among teammates. But for cross-team comparisons—especially defensive value—WS has known limitations.
Can you unpack the defensive side of Win Shares a bit more? I’ve heard it’s not really measuring “defense” the way people think.

Defensive Win Shares (DWS) is one of the most controversial components because it leans on team defensive efficiency and minutes, with limited individual defensive box-score data. A simplified description: 1) Start with team defensive rating (points allowed per 100 possessions). 2) Convert that into “defensive points saved” relative to league average, scaled by possessions/minutes. 3) Allocate those defensive points saved to players primarily based on minutes played, with some adjustments involving defensive rebounds, steals, blocks, and fouls (depending on the implementation details and era). The core issue: the team’s defensive performance is the anchor. Individual defensive events in the box score are sparse and noisy, so the model uses what it has, but it can’t directly observe things like: - On-ball containment - Help rotations and rim deterrence - Screen navigation - Communication and scheme execution - Forcing low-value shots without recording a steal/block As a result, DWS often behaves like: “Were you on the floor a lot for a good defense?” → you get a lot of DWS. That doesn’t mean it’s useless; it can reflect that certain players are part of effective defensive units. But as an individual defender ranking tool, it’s pretty shaky.
So PER is box-score per-minute, WS is team-wins allocated from box-score and team efficiency. Where does BPM fit into this taxonomy?

BPM (Box Plus/Minus) is still box-score based, but it’s explicitly trying to estimate plus-minus impact per 100 possessions—i.e., how a player affects team point differential when on the floor—using a regression trained on historical data. Where PER is “crafted weights,” BPM is “statistical model fitted to outcomes.” Mechanically (high-level): - The target variable is adjusted plus-minus-like impact (or a proxy for it). - Inputs are box-score stats (points, assists, rebounds, steals, blocks, turnovers, etc.), position estimates, and team adjustments. - The model learns coefficients that best predict impact across many seasons. - BPM outputs an estimate of points per 100 possessions above/below average. Strengths: - It’s calibrated to plus-minus impact, not just box-score “production.” - It tries to incorporate positional context (what’s expected from a guard vs center). - It can be more aligned with real on-court value than PER or WS in many cases. Limitations: - Still box-score only: can’t see defense beyond events, can’t see off-ball contributions. - Regression can bake in historical biases (e.g., if certain roles historically correlated with impact, it might over/undervalue players as the game changes). - It can be sensitive to how positions are estimated and to the training data era.
When people talk about “impact metrics” like RAPM, that’s not box score at all, right? How does RAPM actually work?

Right—RAPM (Regularized Adjusted Plus-Minus) is based on lineup data and point differentials, not box score. Core idea: - Every possession (or stint) has 10 players on the floor. - You observe the net points scored by the offense minus defense while those 10 are on the court. - You set up a giant regression problem: estimate each player’s contribution to point differential, controlling for the other 9 players in the stint. Why “adjusted”: - Raw plus-minus is confounded by teammates and opponents. - Adjusted plus-minus tries to isolate individual impact by controlling for who else was on the floor. Why “regularized”: - The regression is ill-conditioned because players share the floor with many of the same teammates; the matrix has multicollinearity. - Regularization (ridge regression typically) shrinks coefficients toward zero to reduce variance and prevent extreme, noisy estimates. Outputs: - A per-100-possessions impact estimate (often split into offense and defense in some variants). Strengths: - Captures everything that affects scoring margins: defense, spacing, screening, decision-making, gravity—anything that shows up in point differential. - Doesn’t rely on box-score proxies. Weaknesses: - Noisy in small samples; needs lots of possessions. - Regularization introduces bias (shrinks true extremes). - Sensitive to lineup usage patterns and coaching (who plays with whom). - Harder to interpret causally because it’s still observational.
How do modern public metrics like EPM or LEBRON relate to RAPM? Are they just “RAPM plus box score”?

That’s a good approximation. Many modern one-number public metrics are “hybrids”: - Start with a plus-minus backbone (APM/RAPM-style) using play-by-play and lineup data. - Stabilize it by blending in priors from box-score and tracking-style features (where available). - Add context adjustments (strength of teammates/opponents, luck adjustments, etc.). - Produce a more stable estimate in-season than pure RAPM. EPM (Estimated Plus-Minus), LEBRON, DARKO, RAPTOR (historically), and others each have different modeling choices, but the general pattern is: - Use multi-year data to stabilize. - Use Bayesian or regularized frameworks. - Use box score as informative prior, especially early in season. - Output both offense and defense components and a total. So yes: “RAPM plus informed priors” is close, though each metric’s secret sauce differs (feature set, regularization, aging curves, possession weighting, etc.).
Let’s go back to PER. People often say it’s “per-minute” but then it’s also pace-adjusted. Isn’t that redundant?

They’re related but not redundant. - Per-minute controls for playing time: how much box-score stuff you produce per minute. - Pace adjustment controls for how many possessions occur per minute in your team environment. Two players could both play 30 minutes, but if one plays in a 102 possessions/game environment and the other in a 94 possessions/game environment, the faster environment naturally generates more opportunities per minute for counting stats—more shots, more rebounds, more assists, etc. PER tries to neutralize that by adjusting to a league-average pace. So per-minute removes “minutes played” effects; pace adjustment removes “possessions per minute” effects. You can think of it as trying to approximate “per possession” productivity, but it’s not a true per-possession stat because it’s still built from per-minute box score events and team pace proxies.
Is PER at least good as a quick offensive summary? Or does it fail even there?

As a quick summary of box-score offensive productivity per minute, it’s not terrible—especially if you keep the caveats in mind. What it does reasonably well: - Rewards efficient scoring and free throws. - Rewards assists and offensive rebounds. - Penalizes misses and turnovers. Where it can mislead offensively: - It doesn’t distinguish shot creation difficulty. A self-created shot diet is harder than assisted finishes, but PER doesn’t know. - It doesn’t incorporate spacing gravity. A shooter who bends defenses but takes fewer shots can be undervalued. - It treats assists as the main playmaking signal, missing advantage creation that doesn’t end in an assist. - It can over-reward high-volume scoring even when the marginal value of that scoring is lower than it appears (e.g., if it comes at the expense of better team offense). So: “okay for a rough per-minute box-score offense read,” but not a definitive offensive ranking.
Suppose I want to do a sanity check: what stats tend to drive PER the most? Like, if I were to do a sensitivity analysis, what’s the biggest lever?

Biggest levers in practice tend to be: 1) Scoring volume with decent efficiency Points are a large component; made shots and free throws add, missed shots subtract. High usage with above-average true shooting generally pushes PER up a lot. 2) Turnovers Turnovers are a strong negative. High-assist but high-turnover creators can get their PER dragged down relative to their actual offensive value. 3) Rebounds (especially defensive rebounds in raw volume) Rebounding adds steady positive value. Big rebounding games noticeably move PER. 4) Blocks and steals They’re less frequent, but the per-event credit is meaningful. A player who gets a lot of steals can see PER rise noticeably. Blocks similarly, though blocks are more position-concentrated. 5) Minutes (indirectly via per-minute stability) Because it’s per-minute, small samples can create extreme PERs. But the “lever” is really variance: limited minutes can yield unstable PER. If you literally perturb a player’s box line: - Add 2 made free throws: PER rises modestly. - Add 2 turnovers: PER drops more noticeably. - Convert 2 missed shots into 2 made shots: big swing (you remove negative and add positive).
How does PER treat three-point shooting? I know it’s in FG and points, but does it explicitly value threes more?

PER’s original formulation does include a term for three-pointers made (3PM) beyond just counting them as field goals, effectively giving extra credit for the added point. But it’s not as explicitly “expected points per shot” optimized as modern efficiency frameworks. In practice: - A three-pointer made counts as a made field goal (good), adds points (good), and often has an extra 3PM component (good). - A three-pointer missed counts as a missed field goal (bad). So it does reward three-point shooting, but it can still understate the spacing/geometry value of high-volume three-point threats who take fewer other box-score actions. It also doesn’t explicitly incorporate shot location efficiency beyond the fact that threes are worth more points.
Let’s compare PER to TS% and usage. Is there a relationship where PER is basically “usage * efficiency” plus some side dishes?

That’s a useful mental model. A big chunk of PER variation is explained by: - Usage/shot attempts and free throws (how many scoring chances you take) - Efficiency (how well you convert them) - Turnovers (the “cost” of usage) - Rebounds (extra possessions) - Assists (creation credit) So yes, you can loosely think: PER ≈ scoring volume adjusted by efficiency + playmaking credit + rebounding + defensive events − turnovers/misses/fouls If you plotted PER against a combination like: (Points per minute) + (assist rate) + (rebound rate) − (turnover rate), with efficiency baked in, you’d get a strong relationship. But PER isn’t identical to usage*efficiency because: - It’s per minute, not per possession. - It credits non-scoring events a lot (rebounds, blocks). - It penalizes misses separately from efficiency metrics like TS%, which already incorporate misses.
One thing I’ve always wondered: why normalize PER to league average 15? Why not just output the raw number?

Normalization is mainly for interpretability and comparability. - A fixed anchor (league average = 15) makes it easy to read: 20 is “well above average,” 10 is “below average.” - It helps comparisons across seasons because the raw unadjusted formula would drift with changes in league shooting, pace, turnover rates, and stat distribution. - It also makes PER feel like a familiar “rating scale.” But normalization can obscure that the league’s distribution can change. For example, if the league becomes more efficient overall, the same raw production might correspond to a different normalized PER depending on the season’s baseline.

Okay, now I want to get very concrete. If I have a player with high points, low assists, high rebounds, decent efficiency, and low turnovers, PER loves him. But what types of players does PER systematically underrate?

Common “PER-underrated” archetypes: 1) Low-usage connectors Players who keep the offense flowing, make quick decisions, and create advantage without racking up assists. They might have modest points and assist totals, low turnovers, but their value is in sequencing and spacing. 2) Elite perimeter defenders who don’t get steals/blocks If a defender’s impact is shot suppression, containment, and forcing tough attempts without generating many steals, PER won’t see it. 3) Floor spacers with gravity High-volume three-point threats who open lanes for others but may not rebound, assist, or score in huge volume can look mediocre in PER. 4) Screeners and roll gravity bigs who don’t rebound as much If a big’s value is in screening and creating openings, PER only sees the finishes and rebounds, not the screening value. 5) Players with “assist-adjacent” creation Passes that lead to free throws, extra passes, or hockey assists don’t count. Some primary creators who generate a lot of team offense may not get full credit if the ball moves after their pass. 6) Players in low-minute, high-impact defensive roles Because PER is per-minute, you might think it helps them—but if their impact comes from defense that doesn’t show up in steals/blocks, it won’t.
On the flip side, what are the classic “PER darlings”?

Typical PER darlings: 1) High-usage scorers with okay-to-good efficiency Even if their defense is weak, the offensive box score can drive PER. 2) High-rebound bigs Especially those who get lots of defensive rebounds and finish efficiently. 3) Block-heavy rim protectors Even when blocks aren’t perfectly aligned with overall defensive value, PER gives them strong credit. 4) Bench bigs with per-minute production High energy rebounders/finishers in limited minutes can post very high PER. 5) Players who rack up steals Steals are valuable, and PER “sees” them clearly.
If I wanted to build a better PER, still box-score only, what would you change?

If constrained to box score only, improvements would mostly be about better aligning weights with possession value and role context: 1) Use possession-based scaling directly Instead of per-minute + pace adjustment, build it per possession using estimated possessions used/created. 2) Use TS% and shot volume more cleanly Separate scoring efficiency from volume in a way that matches points-per-shot value; penalize inefficient volume more sharply. 3) Better turnover context via usage Penalize turnovers relative to on-ball responsibility (e.g., turnovers per usage) rather than absolute per minute. 4) Rebalance rebounding Credit offensive rebounds more than defensive rebounds, and potentially scale defensive rebound credit by team rebound context (hard publicly). 5) Reduce block overweighting Blocks should matter, but less as a standalone; steals are generally more directly possession-ending. 6) Add assist value in a way that accounts for teammate shooting Hard without play-by-play, but you can at least scale assists by team shooting efficiency or use estimated assists created. 7) Positional baselines Compare players to positional averages (guards vs wings vs bigs) to reduce structural bias. 8) Role/usage adjustments Acknowledge that high-usage roles have diminishing returns and more defensive attention; box score alone can’t fully do this, but you can build nonlinear scoring value. At that point you’re drifting toward BPM-style modeling—using regression to learn weights rather than hand-crafting.
Speaking of nonlinear scoring value: can you explain diminishing returns of usage in a way that connects to these metrics?

Diminishing returns of usage is the idea that each additional shot attempt or possession used by the same player is often less efficient than the earlier ones, because: - Defenses focus more attention on high-usage players. - The player is forced into tougher shots later in the possession or later in the game. - The “best” opportunities are taken first; marginal opportunities are worse. In metrics: - PER tends to assume linear value: more points (with efficiency) is more value, without modeling that the 30th point might be harder but also might come with opportunity cost (taking touches from an even more efficient teammate). - Team offense might be maximized by distributing usage to optimize overall efficiency. - Impact metrics implicitly capture this because if a player’s high usage is hurting team efficiency, the lineup point differential will reflect it. So diminishing returns is partly why “box-score production” doesn’t map 1:1 to “team impact,” especially for high-volume scorers.
Let’s talk about Win Shares vs BPM as evaluation tools. If I’m building a model to predict team success, which is more useful?

For predicting team success, you’d typically prefer metrics that correlate with point differential and future performance. - Win Shares is partially constructed from team success already, so it can correlate strongly with wins in-sample, but that can be tautological. As a predictor, it can be okay, but it may not add independent information beyond team efficiency. - BPM is designed to estimate point differential impact from box score, so it can be useful as a player-level predictor. Summing BPM (with minutes weights) across a roster can approximate team strength, though you’d want to be careful because BPM is relative to league average and includes some team adjustments. In practice, for prediction: - Use a blend: minutes-weighted impact estimates (BPM or an impact metric) plus team-level shooting variance, schedule strength, and continuity. - If you only have box score, BPM-like models tend to be better than PER/WS for predicting future point differential.
You mentioned that WS defense is team-anchored. Is offensive Win Shares also team-anchored in a problematic way?

Offensive Win Shares (OWS) is less problematic than DWS, but it still has team anchoring. OWS is built from: - Team offensive efficiency (points scored per possession) - Individual offensive contributions via box score (efficient scoring, assists, offensive rebounds, etc.) Because the team’s offensive efficiency sets the “pool” of offensive value, players on elite offenses may be credited more. However, offense is more directly captured by box score than defense is, so the allocation tends to be more plausible than DWS. Still, issues remain: - Teammate dependence: assists depend on teammates making shots; efficiency can be influenced by shot quality created by others. - Role dependence: a player’s efficiency may be inflated by playing alongside elite creators; OWS might treat that as the player’s own doing. - System effects: spacing and scheme influence everyone’s efficiency, and OWS has limited ability to disentangle.
How do you think about “descriptive” vs “predictive” vs “causal” metrics in basketball analytics?

Useful framing: 1) Descriptive metrics They summarize what happened in observed data. Examples: PER, WS, TS%, assist rate, on/off splits. They can be great for describing production but not necessarily stable or transferable. 2) Predictive metrics They aim to forecast future performance or outcomes. Examples: projection systems (DARKO-style), aging curves, Bayesian priors, models that incorporate multi-year data. They trade off responsiveness for stability. 3) Causal metrics (hardest) They aim to estimate what would happen if you changed something—e.g., swapped a player onto a different team, or increased minutes. True causal inference is tough because you don’t have randomized assignments. Adjusted plus-minus methods are closer to causal than box score, but still observational and sensitive to confounding. Most public “impact” metrics are best seen as: - Descriptive of on-court impact in the sample - Somewhat predictive when stabilized with priors - Not fully causal in the strict sense

I want a worked example conceptually. Suppose two players have identical PER, say 18. One is a high-usage guard with mediocre defense, the other is a low-usage center with high rebounds and blocks. How could their true impact differ?

Even with the same PER, their impact could differ because PER’s mapping from box score to wins/point differential is imperfect and role-dependent. High-usage guard: - Could be creating a large share of team offense against set defenses, which is scarce and valuable. - Might have higher turnover burden and lower efficiency due to shot difficulty, but still be essential. - Defense might be a negative that PER can’t fully capture. Low-usage center: - Might be finishing plays created by others (high efficiency) and collecting rebounds/blocks. - If blocks don’t translate to overall defensive suppression (e.g., sells out for blocks), defensive impact could be less than box score suggests. - Rebounding might be partly team/system driven. So impact outcomes: - The guard might have higher offensive impact than PER suggests (because creation value isn’t fully captured) but lower defensive impact. - The center might have lower offensive creation value than PER suggests (because finishing is easier than creating) but could have high defensive impact if rim protection is real and not just blocks. An impact metric might rate the guard higher offensively and the center higher defensively; total could go either way depending on actual lineup results.
What about “on/off” numbers? People throw around on/off net rating as if it’s definitive. Where does that fit?

On/off net rating is a simple impact proxy: - On-court net rating: team point differential per 100 possessions with the player on the floor. - Off-court net rating: same when the player is off. - On/off = on − off. Pros: - Captures everything (box score + non-box contributions) insofar as it affects scoring margin. - Simple and intuitive. Cons: - Extremely confounded by lineup context: who replaces the player, which lineups they play with, opponent rotations. - Sensitive to small sample and noise (shooting variance). - Coaching patterns can bias it (star staggers, bench units, etc.). On/off is descriptive and can be suggestive, but it’s not adjusted. RAPM-style methods are essentially “adjusted on/off” via regression.
Can you explain why multicollinearity is such a problem in APM/RAPM? I get the term in general stats, but why is it severe here?

In APM, each player is a variable in a regression, and each stint provides an observation of net points with a set of players on the floor. Multicollinearity is severe because: - Teammates share many minutes together, especially starters. - Some players almost always appear in the same lineup combinations. - That means the data doesn’t provide enough independent variation to separate their effects. Example: If Player A and Player B play together almost all the time, and the team is +8 in those minutes, the model can’t easily determine whether: - A is +6 and B is +2, or - A is +2 and B is +6, or - both are +4, etc. Regularization (RAPM) shrinks coefficients and stabilizes, but it also means the estimates are partly determined by the regularization prior, not purely by data.
How do these metrics handle garbage time? Is it typically removed?

Many serious implementations of plus-minus-based metrics filter or down-weight garbage time because: - Rotations change drastically. - Incentives and strategies shift. - The signal about true team strength is weaker. Public box-score metrics like PER/WS generally don’t remove garbage time explicitly; they just take the stats as recorded. That can slightly inflate bench players who get disproportionate low-leverage minutes, though it’s usually a small effect over a season. For impact metrics, garbage time filtering is more common and can matter more because the lineup data in blowouts can be unrepresentative.
Let’s switch to the “stat-head” debate: why do some analysts still cite PER at all if it has these issues?

A few reasons: 1) Availability and familiarity PER has been around a long time, is widely available, and many fans/media recognize it. 2) Quick descriptive snapshot If you want a rough per-minute box-score productivity number, PER gives you that quickly. 3) Historical comparison within a narrow scope For certain questions—like “who had huge box-score rate production in this era?”—PER can be a consistent lens, if you accept its biases. 4) It correlates with other things people care about PER correlates with usage, scoring, and often with recognition (All-Star/All-NBA), so it can “feel” right even if it’s not measuring impact well. 5) It’s a gateway metric People start with PER, then learn why it fails, then graduate to better tools. It can be pedagogically useful if framed correctly.
If PER is “box-score productivity,” what’s the best single box-score stat today? Or is that a wrong question?

It’s not wrong, but it depends on what you want. If you want a single number from box score that best approximates impact, many analysts prefer: - BPM (and derivatives) over PER. - Or box-score versions of plus-minus estimators (like “BPM-style” models) because they’re trained to match impact. If you want a clean efficiency stat: - TS% for scoring efficiency, plus usage to contextualize. If you want a box-score “value” estimate: - BPM is usually a better “one-number” than PER because it’s calibrated to plus-minus outcomes. But the best practice is not to rely on a single number: - Use TS% + usage + turnover rate + assist rate + rebound rates + defensive event rates. - Then supplement with impact metrics for the “everything else” component.
Can you give an example of how BPM tries to account for position?

BPM includes a positional adjustment because the same box score line can mean different things depending on role. For example: - 8 rebounds and 2 blocks from a center might be closer to “expected,” whereas from a guard it’s unusual and potentially more valuable signal (though blocks for guards are rare). - 7 assists from a point guard is more expected than from a center; but for a center, that might signal unusual playmaking. So BPM estimates a player’s “position” based on their stat profile (or listed position plus heuristics) and then applies coefficients that reflect how those stats historically translate to plus-minus for that position. This helps reduce the “big man bias” in pure box-score aggregation because it recognizes that rebounds are more common and less differentiating among bigs.
But doesn’t that risk encoding stereotypes? Like if the league changes and bigs start passing more, does BPM lag?

Yes, that’s a real concern. If the model is trained on historical data where: - bigs who pass a lot were rare and correlated with certain team contexts, then the coefficient for “center assists” might be estimated in a way that doesn’t generalize perfectly as the league evolves. Modern models try to mitigate this by: - Updating training data regularly. - Using more flexible feature sets. - Sometimes using multi-year rolling windows to capture evolution. Still, any regression-based metric can lag structural changes, which is why analysts cross-check with: - play-by-play impact - lineup data - film-based evaluations (even if you’re a stat-head, you can treat film as a qualitative data source)
Let’s do a deeper dive on Win Shares. How does it translate points to wins? Is it basically Pythagorean expectation?

It’s related. The general approach is: 1) Estimate team wins from team point differential / offensive and defensive efficiency. A Pythagorean expectation formula estimates winning percentage as a function of points scored and points allowed (or per-possession versions). It’s not exactly the same in all implementations, but the spirit is: point differential maps to wins nonlinearly. 2) Determine “marginal wins” and “marginal points.” Dean Oliver’s framework uses the concept of marginal points produced vs allowed relative to league average. Those marginal points are converted into marginal wins using a points-per-win factor that depends on league environment. 3) Allocate marginal points to players. Offensive marginal points are allocated based on individual offensive contributions; defensive marginal points are allocated based on minutes and team defense proxies. Then: Win Shares = (marginal points) / (points per win) So yes: it’s a point-to-win translation plus an allocation scheme.
Is the points-per-win constant stable?

It varies by league environment (pace, scoring levels, variance in team strengths). In many seasons, a rough rule of thumb is around 30–35 points of point differential per win over an 82-game season (so about 2.5–3 points per game per win). But the exact factor can shift. Win Shares uses season-specific context to calibrate that conversion, which is good. However, even if the conversion is right at the team level, the allocation to individuals is the harder part.
If I want to evaluate a player on a bad team, what do you recommend using so I don’t under-credit them?

A few approaches: 1) Rate stats and efficiency Use TS%, turnover rate, usage, assist rate, and shot creation proxies (like free throw rate) to see if the player is doing difficult, valuable offense efficiently. 2) Impact metrics with adjustments Use multi-year RAPM-style metrics or stabilized public impact metrics (EPM/LEBRON-type) that adjust for teammates/opponents. These are designed to be more team-context-robust than WS. 3) On/off with lineup context If you use on/off, look at: - Who they share minutes with - Who replaces them - Strength of opponent lineups faced It’s more work, but it can prevent misreads. 4) Role-aware comparisons Compare the player to others in similar roles (usage and position) rather than to all players. 5) Projections If the question is “how good is this player going forward,” use projection systems that incorporate priors and aging rather than just current-year outcomes.
Back to PER flaws: how much is “defense invisibility” vs “bad weights”?

Defense invisibility is the big one, because even perfect weights on steals/blocks wouldn’t capture most defense. But “bad weights” matter too, because: - If you overweight rebounds, you inflate certain roles. - If you treat all assists similarly, you miss context. - If you penalize turnovers without adjusting for creation burden, you distort guard value. So it’s both: - The missing variable problem (unobserved contributions) - The mis-specified model problem (weights not matching true marginal value)
I’ve seen people say PER is basically obsolete because of tracking data. But public tracking data is limited. Is PER still useful for historical analysis?

For historical analysis, PER can still be used as one lens, with caution. Pros historically: - Consistent availability across many seasons. - Doesn’t require play-by-play data (which is limited for older eras). - Captures a certain type of box-score dominance. Cons historically: - Era differences in stat recording (assists, blocks, steals—especially before certain stats were tracked). - Changes in pace and shot profiles can alter what “dominance” looks like. - Defensive evaluation is especially limited historically. So: PER can help identify “who had huge box-score rate production,” but it shouldn’t be treated as definitive “best player” evidence, especially cross-era.
Let’s talk about correlation. How well does PER correlate with team net rating when you minutes-weight it across a roster?

Generally, PER correlates moderately with team success because good teams have good players who put up good box-score lines. But it’s not especially strong as a team-strength predictor compared to: - minutes-weighted BPM - multi-year impact metrics - or simply team offensive/defensive efficiency itself The correlation is limited by: - PER’s defensive blindness - the fact that team performance depends on fit and lineup interactions - the nonlinearity of roles (a team needs certain skills; stacking high-PER players doesn’t guarantee synergy) If you did a regression predicting team net rating from the sum of minutes-weighted PER above average, you’d get some signal, but it would be weaker and noisier than more impact-aligned metrics.
How do you handle “fit” analytically? Like, two players can each be good, but together they’re less than the sum of parts.

Fit is about interaction effects—how player skills complement or overlap. Analytically, you can approach it via: 1) Lineup data Look at the net rating of combinations: - Pair on-court vs each separately - Use adjusted lineup models to estimate synergy (hard, noisy) 2) Skill complementarity proxies Spacing + rim pressure, playmaking + finishing, etc. Even without tracking data, you can infer: - shooting gravity proxies (3PA rate, accuracy) - rim pressure (FT rate, rim attempts if available) - playmaking (assist rate, potential assists if available) - defensive versatility proxies (foul rate, steal rate, block rate, rebound rate, positional estimates) 3) Shot profile interactions If both players need the same zones, overlap reduces value; if one expands spacing, it increases value. 4) Diminishing returns within role Two on-ball creators might cannibalize usage; one needs to scale off-ball to maintain value. Most single-number metrics ignore interaction terms. Fit is where you move from “player value” to “roster construction.”
I’m curious: is there a way to “decompose” PER into components like offense and defense like BPM does?

PER doesn’t naturally decompose into clean offense/defense because its defensive inputs are limited and mixed in with overall box-score events. You can approximate a decomposition by grouping terms: - Offensive-ish: scoring (FG, FT, misses), assists, turnovers, offensive rebounds - Defensive-ish: defensive rebounds, steals, blocks, personal fouls But it won’t align with true offense/defense impact. For example: - Defensive rebounds are partly defensive, partly “end of possession” credit, and partly team context. - Steals are defense but also create offense (transition). - Blocks vary in whether they retain possession. BPM and impact metrics are better for offense/defense splits because they’re trained/constructed to map to point differential components.
How much does PER penalize missed shots? Is it equivalent to TS%?

PER penalizes missed shots via negative terms for FGA minus FGM (misses) and for missed free throws, but it’s not the same as TS%. TS% is essentially: Points / (2*(FGA + 0.44*FTA)) It measures scoring efficiency per shooting possession. PER’s penalty for misses is embedded in a larger additive formula. It “counts” a miss as a negative event rather than directly computing efficiency per shot. This can lead to differences: - Two players with the same TS% but different shot volumes and free throw rates might be treated differently in PER. - The marginal penalty per miss in PER might not perfectly match the possession value implied by TS%. So PER is related to efficiency but not equivalent.
Let’s do an argument I see online: “PER hates high-volume three-point shooters because misses are punished.” Is that true?

Not in a simple way. - PER penalizes misses, yes. - But it also rewards made threes (as makes + points + sometimes explicit 3PM credit). So if a high-volume three-point shooter is efficient (good TS%), PER generally likes them. The issue is more about players who: - take lots of threes at average-ish efficiency - don’t contribute rebounds/assists/steals - have low free throw rates Those players can have high spacing value but modest PER because PER doesn’t credit spacing; it only credits the made shots and penalizes misses. A player can be valuable by stretching defenses even if their raw box score is modest.
Can we talk about “replacement level” and “value over replacement”? Win Shares feels like it’s doing something like that. Is it?

Win Shares is somewhat aligned with “value” but it’s not explicitly replacement-level in the way that some baseball metrics are. - WS is anchored to team wins and marginal points, which is closer to “value above average” allocated within a team context. - There is an implicit baseline in the marginal points framework (often league average efficiency), but it’s not a clean replacement-level baseline. Some basketball metrics explicitly use replacement level: - VORP (Value Over Replacement Player) is derived from BPM and uses a replacement-level baseline to estimate cumulative value above that threshold. - Some impact metrics also provide “wins above replacement” style outputs. Replacement-level framing is useful because it ties value to roster decisions: how much better is this player than a readily available alternative?
How does VORP relate to BPM precisely?

VORP is essentially BPM converted into a cumulative “value” number over minutes, relative to a replacement-level baseline. Conceptually: - Start with BPM (points per 100 possessions above/below average). - Define replacement level as some negative BPM (because replacement players are below average). - Convert BPM above replacement into “points above replacement” using possessions played (minutes adjusted by pace). - Convert points above replacement into wins using a points-per-win factor. So: VORP ≈ (BPM − replacement baseline) * possessions played / (points per win) Exact constants vary, but that’s the pipeline.
That makes sense. In that case, PER is missing the “minutes played” aspect for cumulative value, right?

PER is a rate stat; it doesn’t incorporate minutes directly. You can create a cumulative version by multiplying PER above average by minutes, but it’s not standard, and it wouldn’t map cleanly to wins. Win Shares is cumulative by construction. BPM has both a rate interpretation and can be turned into cumulative value (VORP). So yes: PER alone doesn’t tell you “total season value.” A player with PER 20 in limited minutes could be less valuable than a player with PER 17 in heavy minutes.
Let’s talk about defensive metrics. If box score is limited, what’s the best public approach for defense?

Publicly, the best approach is usually: - Use an impact metric’s defensive component (e.g., defensive EPM/LEBRON-type) because it’s based on lineup outcomes. - Cross-check with role and matchup context if available. - Use defensive play-by-play proxies cautiously (opponent shooting, etc.)—but those are noisy and heavily confounded. Box-score-only defensive metrics (like DBPM, DWS) can be useful as rough signals but are often misleading for individual defense. If you’re forced to use box score: - Steal rate is often a better signal than block rate for perimeter defense. - Block rate matters for rim protection but is incomplete. - Defensive rebound rate is partially informative but heavily role-based. Ultimately, defense is where impact metrics add the most value over box score.
I’ve seen debates about whether RAPM defense is reliable. Can you address that?

RAPM defense is more reliable than box-score defense in principle because it captures all effects on points allowed. But it’s still noisy and sensitive. Reasons it can be unreliable: - Defense has more shared responsibility; individual signal is weaker. - Lineup patterns can confound: a defender might always play with a great rim protector, making them look better. - Opponent shooting variance adds noise. - Scheme effects: a team scheme can make individuals look good or bad in plus-minus. Ways to improve reliability: - Use multi-year samples. - Use priors (hybrid metrics). - Separate “luck” components (opponent shot-making variance) from process where possible. So: RAPM defense is valuable but should be treated probabilistically (with error bars), not as an exact ranking.
Error bars are a good point. Most public metrics don’t show uncertainty. How should a stat-head think about that?

Treat point estimates as ranges, especially for defense and small samples. Rules of thumb: - Early season: huge uncertainty; priors dominate in good models. - Single season: moderate uncertainty; still noisy for many players. - Multi-year: better, but still not perfect. Also: - Differences of, say, 0.5 points per 100 possessions in an impact metric are often within noise for many players. - Big gaps (like 3–4 points) are more meaningful. If a metric doesn’t publish uncertainty, you can infer it from: - minutes played - stability across seasons - agreement across different metrics Consensus across multiple independent metrics is more convincing than one number.
Let’s do a practical workflow. If I’m evaluating a player, what’s your step-by-step stat pipeline?

A solid stat-head pipeline: 1) Role and minutes - Usage, minutes, position/role, team context. 2) Scoring profile - TS%, shot volume, free throw rate, three-point rate. - If available: shot location breakdown. 3) Creation and ball security - Assist rate, turnover rate, assist-to-turnover, estimated creation load. 4) Rebounding - Offensive and defensive rebounding rates, contextualized by position. 5) Defensive event rates - Steal rate, block rate, foul rate (with caution). 6) Box-score composite - BPM (or similar) as a box-score impact proxy. - PER/WS only as secondary descriptors. 7) Impact metrics - On/off, then adjusted impact (EPM/LEBRON/RAPM-style) with multi-year view. 8) Fit indicators - Spacing, lineup synergy (if you can), and how skills scale next to stars. 9) Compare across peers - Compare to players with similar roles/usage, not just league-wide ranks. 10) Sanity checks - Does the story make sense across multiple metrics? Any red flags (e.g., huge PER but low impact)?
That’s good. Now, I want to sharpen the critique: what is the strongest argument that PER is “flawed,” not just “limited”?

The strongest argument is that PER’s construction systematically misestimates value because it: - assigns large credit to events that are easier to accumulate in certain roles (defensive rebounds, blocks) without adequately adjusting for context, - fails to account for major sources of value (defense without events, spacing, screening, off-ball play), - and uses fixed weights not empirically optimized for predicting impact. So it’s not merely “missing some nuance.” It can rank players in ways that are directionally wrong for overall impact—especially when comparing across roles (high-usage creators vs finishers; perimeter defenders vs big rebounders). If you’re using it as “best player” evidence, it can lead you astray more than a metric designed to approximate plus-minus impact.
Are there any situations where PER might actually be preferable to BPM?

A few niche cases: 1) Very quick descriptive per-minute box score If you want a simple descriptive number without regression-based assumptions, PER is straightforward. 2) Historical continuity for certain datasets If you’re comparing within an older dataset where BPM isn’t available or consistent, PER might be more accessible. 3) When you explicitly want to avoid plus-minus calibration BPM’s coefficients are trained to match plus-minus, which has its own biases and noise. If you distrust that mapping, you might prefer a purely box-score productivity index. But you’d still want to be careful because PER’s weights are also assumptions. In most modern analysis, BPM is generally more informative for overall value, but PER can still be a descriptive tool.
Let’s talk about “league average is 15” again. Does that mean PER is comparable across positions? Or is it still biased?

Normalizing to league average doesn’t remove positional bias. It just sets the overall mean. If the metric structurally favors certain box-score profiles (e.g., bigs), then: - bigs will tend to cluster above 15 more often than their true impact would warrant, - and certain perimeter roles will cluster below 15. To fix positional bias, you need positional baselines or a model that accounts for role and expected stat distributions (like BPM tries to do).
How do analysts test whether a metric is “good”?

Common tests: 1) Face validity Does it rank players in a way that broadly aligns with informed evaluation? 2) Predictive validity Does it predict future outcomes? - future plus-minus - future team net rating - future wins - future scoring margin 3) Stability / reliability How consistent is it year-to-year for the same player, after controlling for aging and role? 4) Incremental value Does it add predictive power beyond simpler stats (TS%, usage, etc.)? 5) Out-of-sample performance If you train weights, does it generalize to new seasons? PER often scores okay on face validity for offensive production but weaker on predictive and incremental tests compared to modern impact-aligned metrics.
What about “clutch” and leverage? Do these metrics treat all minutes equally?

Most of them treat all minutes roughly equally. - PER: all box-score stats count the same regardless of game state. - WS: based on season-long efficiency; no explicit leverage weighting. - BPM: box-score-based; no leverage. - RAPM/impact: usually treats possessions equally, though some models may filter garbage time. But leverage matters: late-game possessions in close games may be more valuable. The problem is: - leverage-weighted metrics get noisier quickly because the sample shrinks. - players face different opponent strategies in high leverage. So most metrics prioritize stability over leverage weighting. Analysts sometimes do separate “clutch” analyses, but they’re usually descriptive rather than definitive.
If I wanted to build my own metric, would you recommend starting from plus-minus or from box score?

If you have play-by-play lineup data, start from plus-minus and then regularize/blend with box score for stability. That’s the modern path because: - plus-minus captures the full game impact - box score provides informative priors to reduce noise If you only have box score, start from a BPM-style regression: - target: plus-minus or team efficiency differential - features: box-score rates, positional indicators, maybe team context - train out-of-sample, validate, and be transparent about uncertainty Starting from hand-crafted weights (PER-style) is doable but usually inferior to data-driven calibration unless you have a strong theoretical model of possession value.
Give me a conceptual sketch of a BPM-like regression. What’s the dependent variable, what are the features, and what’s the model form?

Conceptual sketch: Dependent variable (target): - Player’s adjusted plus-minus per 100 possessions (or a proxy), often derived from lineup data and then attributed to players. Features (inputs), typically per-100 possessions or per-minute rates: - Scoring: points, FGA, 3PA, FTA, TS% proxies - Playmaking: assists, turnovers - Rebounding: OREB, DREB - Defense events: steals, blocks, fouls - Position estimate (continuous) - Team context controls (sometimes) Model form: - Linear regression is common for interpretability: Impact ≈ β0 + β1*PTS + β2*AST + β3*TRB + β4*STL + β5*BLK − β6*TOV − β7*misses + β8*position + … But there are complexities: - You might use nonlinear terms (usage interactions). - You might include interaction terms (e.g., assists are more valuable at higher usage). - Regularization can prevent overfitting. - You might fit separate offense/defense targets. The key is that coefficients are learned to best predict impact, not chosen ad hoc.
That’s the “modeling” view. But as a consumer of stats, how do I avoid being fooled by a metric that looks sophisticated but is just opaque?

A few heuristics: 1) Ask what data it uses - Box score only? - Lineup plus-minus? - Tracking? More data isn’t automatically better, but it tells you what it can possibly capture. 2) Check validation - Does the creator show out-of-sample predictive tests? - Do they show year-to-year stability? - Do they compare to baselines? 3) Look for transparency about uncertainty Even if they don’t publish error bars, do they discuss noise, sample size, and limitations? 4) Compare across independent metrics If multiple unrelated approaches agree, confidence rises. 5) Watch for extreme claims If a metric consistently produces counterintuitive extremes without strong justification, it might be overfitting or mis-specified. 6) Use it as one input Even the best metric should be part of a portfolio, not the entire evaluation.
Okay, bring it back to the original: if I’m in an argument and someone cites PER as proof a player is better, what’s the clean rebuttal without sounding like a snob?

A clean rebuttal: “PER is a pace-adjusted, per-minute box-score productivity stat. It’s useful for summarizing certain box-score contributions, but it misses most defense and a lot of off-ball value, and it tends to favor high-usage scorers and big rebound/block guys. If we want overall impact, we should look at metrics calibrated to plus-minus (like BPM or an impact metric) and also check efficiency and role.” That keeps it factual, acknowledges PER’s utility, and redirects to better evidence.
Nice. Now, can you compare PER and WS in a single sentence each, like a TL;DR?

PER: “A pace-adjusted per-minute box-score productivity index normalized to league average 15.” Win Shares: “A cumulative allocation of team wins to players based on estimated offensive and defensive contributions derived from box score and team efficiency.”
Let’s go beyond the usual. Are there any weird edge cases where PER gives a bizarre result?

A few edge cases: 1) Per-minute monsters in tiny samples A player in limited minutes can have extreme PER due to hot shooting, high rebound rate, or a couple steals/blocks. It can look absurdly high without meaning “star.” 2) Players with low turnovers due to low responsibility If a player never handles the ball, they avoid turnover penalties and can look cleaner in PER than a creator who takes risks. 3) Rebound-heavy games with low offensive involvement A player can have a strong PER from rebounds and blocks even if their offensive role is minimal, which can overstate overall value. 4) Players who foul a lot Fouls are penalized, but sometimes players with high foul rates can still have high PER if they pile up other stats. The net can be misleading because foul trouble affects availability and team strategy in ways PER doesn’t model. 5) “Empty” steals/blocks If a player gambles and gets steals but gives up structure, PER sees steals but not the breakdowns.
For Win Shares, what’s the weird edge case?

For WS: 1) Team-defense passengers A player logging heavy minutes on an elite defense can rack up DWS even if their individual defense is not particularly strong. 2) Minutes inflation Because WS is cumulative, durable players who play a lot can accumulate more WS than higher-quality players with fewer minutes, even if per-minute impact is better. 3) System-inflated efficiency Players on elite offenses can get high OWS due to efficient shot opportunities created by others, and WS may attribute too much credit to the finisher. 4) Cross-team comparability issues Because WS is team-anchored, comparing a player on a 60-win team to one on a 35-win team can be misleading.
I want to talk about “assist credit.” PER and WS both reward assists, but assists are a messy stat. How do modern analysts handle that?

Modern analysts treat assists as one signal among several, and they often prefer: - play-by-play-derived creation metrics: potential assists, assist points created - usage + efficiency of teammates’ shots after passes (if available) - “gravity” and spacing indicators for off-ball creators In public box-score-only contexts, you can at least: - look at assist rate relative to usage (creation efficiency) - consider turnover rate (risk) - consider team shooting and scheme (assist conversion) Assist numbers alone can mislead because: - a great passer with poor shooting teammates gets fewer assists - a player in a system with lots of “one-pass then shoot” gets more assists - scorekeeper tendencies affect counts Impact metrics can capture playmaking value even when assists don’t, because lineups score more efficiently with the player.
Does PER double-count scoring in a way? Like points and made shots both get credit?

PER’s scoring component is constructed from box-score events, and points are implicitly represented through made field goals and made free throws, with adjustments for threes. It’s not exactly “double-counting” because the formula is designed around those events, but it can feel like it because: - made shots contribute to points and also appear as made shots - the formula is event-based, not purely points-based In practice, it’s a crafted index, so whether it “double-counts” depends on how you interpret the coefficients. The intention is to assign appropriate marginal value to each event, but because it’s not derived from a clean possession-value model, it can overemphasize certain scoring aspects.
If you had to pick one: PER, WS/48, BPM, or on/off net rating—what’s the best “quick stat” and why?

If I have to pick one quick stat for overall value, I’d pick BPM because: - it’s box-score based (widely available) - it’s designed to approximate plus-minus impact - it’s rate-based and can be contextualized with minutes or VORP On/off net rating can be great but is highly context-dependent and noisy. WS/48 is tied to team success and can be misleading defensively. PER is descriptive but more biased.
How should I interpret WS/48? Is it like PER where 0.100 is average?

WS/48 is normalized such that: - 0.100 WS/48 is roughly league average (historically). Higher is better, lower is worse. But remember: - It’s still derived from WS, so it inherits team-context issues, especially on defense. - It can be useful for comparing players within similar team contexts or as a broad indicator, but it’s not a pure individual impact measure.
Let’s add another layer: era and rule changes. How do these metrics handle a league that shifts from midrange to threes?

They handle it differently: - PER: Because it’s normalized each season, the league-average anchor adjusts, but the underlying weights may not perfectly reflect the changing marginal value of shot types, spacing, and pace. It will adapt somewhat but not conceptually capture geometry changes. - Win Shares: It uses team efficiency context, so it adapts at the team level, but allocation still relies on box score; shifts in how offense is created can change what box score stats “mean.” - BPM: If updated and retrained, it can adapt as relationships between box score stats and impact change. If not updated, it can lag. - RAPM/impact: It directly measures point differential, so it naturally reflects era changes in how scoring happens. But comparability across eras is tricky because the baseline environment changes; you’d compare relative to league average.
If impact metrics are best, why do people still care about box-score metrics at all?

Because box-score metrics answer different questions and have practical advantages: - Interpretability: You can see what drove the number (points, assists, etc.). - Stability in small samples: Box-score stats can stabilize faster than plus-minus in some respects, especially for offense. - Historical depth: Box scores exist for far more seasons than detailed play-by-play. - Skill diagnosis: Box score helps identify what a player is doing well or poorly; impact metrics tell you “it worked,” not always “why.” Best practice is to use both: - box score for skill profile - impact for overall effect
I want to press on interpretability. Impact metrics say “+3.2,” but that doesn’t tell me what the player does. How do analysts bridge that?

They combine: - Impact estimate (what happens to score margin) with - Skill indicators (how it happens) For offense: - shooting efficiency and volume - shot profile - passing/creation proxies - turnover control For defense: - role (rim protection vs perimeter containment) - event rates (steals/blocks) - team scheme context - sometimes matchup data if available Then they look for alignment: - If impact is high and skills suggest plausible mechanisms, confidence increases. - If impact is high but skills don’t show much, maybe the value is off-ball/scheme/fit—or the metric is noisy. - If skills look great but impact is low, maybe the role doesn’t translate, or the team context is poor, or the sample is noisy.
Let’s do a mini case study hypothetically. Player A: PER 23, BPM 2.0, impact metric +1.5. Player B: PER 17, BPM 4.5, impact metric +4.0. How would you interpret?

Player A: - PER 23 suggests huge box-score per-minute production. - BPM 2.0 suggests that when calibrated to impact, the box score translates to only moderately above-average impact. - Impact +1.5 supports “good but not elite” on-court effect. Interpretation: likely a player whose box score is inflated by rebounds/blocks/finishing or by high usage that doesn’t translate to efficient team offense/defense. Could be a role-dependent producer. Player B: - PER 17 is good but not flashy in box score. - BPM 4.5 and impact +4.0 suggest strong two-way impact not fully captured by PER—possibly defense, spacing, off-ball value, or efficient low-turnover creation. In an evaluation, you’d generally rate Player B as more impactful overall, and you’d dig into why PER underrates them (likely non-box contributions).
That helps. Now, one more technical question: how do these metrics treat offensive rebounds? Are they consistently valued?

Offensive rebounds are generally valued more than defensive rebounds in good models because they: - extend possessions - often lead to high-efficiency putbacks or reset the offense - are less “automatic” than defensive rebounds PER credits offensive rebounds positively; WS includes offensive rebound contributions in offensive value; BPM tends to value them as well because they correlate with extra possessions and plus-minus. The nuance: - Not all offensive rebounds are equal; some come at the expense of transition defense, and some are scheme-dependent (crash vs get back). Box score can’t see that tradeoff well. - Still, as a general rule, offensive rebounds are among the more reliably valuable box-score events.
Do any of these metrics handle “spacing” explicitly?

Not PER or WS directly. BPM only indirectly, via correlations in the training data (e.g., three-point attempts and efficiency). Impact metrics capture spacing effects implicitly because spacing improves lineup scoring margins, but they don’t always attribute it cleanly unless they include shooting gravity features. Some modern proprietary/public hybrids incorporate: - three-point volume as a gravity proxy - tracking-based “defender distance” or “gravity” if available - off-ball movement metrics But with purely public box score, spacing is mostly inferred from 3PA rate and accuracy, which is an imperfect proxy.
If you had to explain to someone why three-point volume matters even if efficiency is the same, what’s the analytic argument?

Even at the same efficiency, higher three-point volume can matter because: - It changes defensive positioning: defenders stay attached, reducing help. - It increases the frequency of “high-variance” shots, which can affect game-to-game outcomes. - It can create more space for teammates, improving their efficiency (a spillover effect not captured by the shooter’s own TS%). - It changes shot distribution: replacing midrange twos with threes can raise expected points even if the shooter’s personal efficiency is similar, depending on what shots are displaced. The key is externality: a shooter’s value isn’t only in points they score, but in how they alter the defense.
Great. Circle back: if PER is flawed, why was it so popular in the first place?

Timing and accessibility. When PER became popular: - Public play-by-play data and lineup-based impact metrics weren’t widely accessible. - Box score was the main available data source. - People wanted a single-number summary beyond points per game. - PER offered a systematic, repeatable, pace-adjusted approach with an easy-to-read scale. So PER filled a real demand. Analytics has since moved toward impact modeling, but PER remains culturally sticky.
Let’s end with a checklist. If I see someone post PER in a debate, what follow-up questions should I ask?

Good follow-ups: 1) What’s the player’s role and usage? 2) How efficient are they (TS%, shot profile)? 3) What’s their turnover rate relative to creation load? 4) How does their BPM (or similar) compare? 5) What do impact metrics say (multi-year if possible)? 6) How does the team perform with them on vs off, and who are their lineup partners? 7) Are there defensive indicators beyond steals/blocks (or impact defense)? 8) Does the player provide spacing or off-ball value that PER misses? That turns the debate from “one number” into a structured evaluation.
Good. I’m going to ask a slightly contrarian question: could PER be “less wrong” than impact metrics in some cases because plus-minus is so noisy?

In small samples, yes—PER can be less noisy than pure plus-minus. If you only have: - a few hundred possessions of lineup data, then plus-minus-based estimates can swing wildly due to shooting variance and opponent quality. Box-score stats can stabilize faster for certain skills (like shooting efficiency over moderate volume, rebounding rates). So in the short term: - PER might be a steadier descriptive indicator of what a player did in the box score. - But “steadier” isn’t the same as “more accurate” for total impact; it’s just less variable. That’s why hybrid metrics exist: they blend plus-minus (captures full impact) with box score priors (stabilizes noise).
If I wanted to approximate a hybrid metric myself without a massive dataset, what’s a simple approach?

A simple DIY hybrid approach: 1) Start with a box-score impact proxy (BPM-like) as your prior estimate for player impact. 2) Compute on/off net rating for the player (with garbage time filtered if possible). 3) Shrink the on/off toward the prior based on minutes: Hybrid = w * on/off + (1 − w) * prior where w increases with minutes played (more minutes → trust on/off more). This is a rough empirical Bayes idea: - prior = stable expectation from box score - data = noisy on/off signal - weight = depends on sample size It won’t be as good as full RAPM, but it’s conceptually aligned with how many modern metrics stabilize.
That’s a cool heuristic. Alright, last thing: summarize the “PER is flawed” argument in a way that’s precise, not just vibes.

PER is flawed as an overall player value metric because it is a pace-adjusted, per-minute box-score index with hand-set weights that (1) cannot observe most defensive and off-ball contributions, (2) structurally over-credits high-frequency, role-dependent stats like rebounds and blocks, (3) under-credits creation and spacing that don’t show up as assists or points, and (4) therefore can mis-rank players across roles relative to their true effect on team point differential and wins.

Can you explain how WAR is calculated? And why do fWAR and bWAR give different numbers for the same player?

WAR (Wins Above Replacement) is an attempt to summarize a player’s total value—batting, baserunning, fielding, and (for pitchers) run prevention—into a single number expressed in wins relative to a “replacement-level” player (a readily available minor leaguer or bench player). At a high level, most WAR frameworks follow the same pipeline: 1) Estimate runs the player contributed or saved versus some baseline (often average). 2) Add a replacement-level adjustment (so “above average” becomes “above replacement”). 3) Convert runs to wins using a runs-per-win factor (depends on scoring environment). 4) Apply positional adjustments (for position players) and league adjustments. 5) Sum across components. Why fWAR and bWAR differ: - They use different inputs for key components (especially defense and pitching). - They implement different run estimators (e.g., FIP-based vs runs-allowed-based pitching). - They may use different park factors, positional adjustments, replacement levels, and run-to-win conversions. - Even when conceptually similar, small methodological differences compound. Concretely: - FanGraphs position-player WAR (“fWAR”) uses offense via wRAA (from wOBA), baserunning (BsR), defense via UZR (historically; more recently also OAA-based variants for some displays), plus positional and replacement adjustments. - Baseball-Reference position-player WAR (“bWAR”) uses offense via batting runs (from linear weights similar in spirit), baserunning runs, and defense via DRS (Defensive Runs Saved), plus positional and replacement adjustments. - FanGraphs pitcher WAR is primarily FIP-based: it evaluates what “should have happened” based on K, BB, HBP, HR (and sometimes IFFB etc depending on era), scaled to runs, then to wins. - Baseball-Reference pitcher WAR is primarily runs-allowed-based (RA9), adjusted for park and defense (and sometimes leverage/role nuances), then to wins. So the same player can have different WARs because (a) different defensive metrics disagree, (b) FIP vs RA9 can diverge for pitchers, and (c) park/positional/replacement modeling differs.
Let’s go step by step. For position players, what are the exact components that go into WAR? I want the breakdown with run units before converting to wins.

For position-player WAR, both major public versions can be understood as: WAR = (Batting Runs + Baserunning Runs + Fielding Runs + Positional Adjustment + League Adjustment + Replacement Runs) / (Runs per Win) The naming differs by site, but the run components are similar. FanGraphs (fWAR) position players (classic formulation): - Batting runs: wRAA (Weighted Runs Above Average) derived from wOBA. - Baserunning: BsR (includes stolen-base runs and “UBR” for other baserunning). - Fielding: UZR (Ultimate Zone Rating), position-specific. - Positional adjustment: a run value per 1,350 defensive innings (roughly a full season) based on position difficulty. - League adjustment: small correction for differences between AL/NL (in DH eras) or overall league quality; often near zero in modern usage but exists in principle. - Replacement runs: a constant per playing time (plate appearances) that sets replacement level below average. Baseball-Reference (bWAR) position players (conceptual): - Batting runs: “Rbat” (batting runs) computed from linear weights (runs above average). - Baserunning: “Rbaser” (includes stolen base and other baserunning). - Grounded into double plays: “Rdp” (runs saved/ lost avoiding GIDP) sometimes treated as part of batting/baserunning mix. - Fielding: “Rfield” based largely on DRS. - Positional adjustment: “Rpos” similar in spirit, scaled by innings at positions. - Replacement runs: “Rrep” based on playing time. - League adjustment: “Rlg” (small). In both systems, each component is in runs. Then they sum to total runs above replacement (RAR). Finally: WAR = RAR / (runs per win) The runs-per-win factor (“RPW”) is typically about 9–10 runs per win in modern MLB, but is computed from the run environment (e.g., runs per game) and sometimes includes a Pythagorean-like slope or empirical conversion.
How do they compute wRAA from wOBA? I know wOBA is like OBP but weighted; I want the exact conversion to runs.

FanGraphs uses a standardized conversion: 1) Compute wOBA for the player: wOBA = (Σ (weight_event * count_event)) / (PA - IBB - SF - HBP? depending on exact wOBA denominator definition for that season) The exact denominator and inclusion of HBP/IBB can vary slightly by year per FanGraphs’ published constants. 2) Convert wOBA to wRAA: wRAA = ((wOBA - league_wOBA) / wOBA_scale) * PA Where: - league_wOBA is the league-average wOBA for that season (and sometimes league, e.g., AL/NL). - wOBA_scale is a seasonal constant that converts points of wOBA into runs per plate appearance. It’s derived so that wOBA maps to the same run scale as linear weights for that year’s run environment. - PA is plate appearances (as defined by FanGraphs; typically AB + BB + HBP + SF + SH + ...; again, definitions matter slightly). Intuition: - (wOBA - lg_wOBA) gives how many “wOBA points” above average the player is. - Divide by wOBA_scale to convert wOBA points into runs per PA. - Multiply by PA to get total runs above average. So wRAA is “runs above average” from batting, context-neutral (not RBI, not lineup-dependent).
Do you have an example with numbers? Like if a player has .370 wOBA in a league with .320 wOBA, scale 1.25, and 650 PA.

Using the formula: wRAA = ((wOBA - lg_wOBA) / wOBA_scale) * PA = ((0.370 - 0.320) / 1.25) * 650 = (0.050 / 1.25) * 650 = 0.040 * 650 = 26.0 runs So that player would be credited with about +26 batting runs above average (wRAA) for the season. If you wanted to move from above average to above replacement, you’d later add replacement runs (a positive number) and include other components like baserunning, fielding, positional adjustment, etc.
Where do replacement runs come from? Is replacement level a fixed number like “.294 winning percentage” or something? How is it translated into runs?

Replacement level is a conceptual baseline: the performance you’d expect from a readily available “free” player (waiver wire, AAA call-up, bench fill-in). Public WAR implementations set replacement level as a fraction of league average, often expressed in wins percentage or runs per plate appearance/inning. A common rule of thumb: - Replacement level team winning percentage ~ .294 (varies by source and era). - That corresponds to being about 48–50 wins worse than average over a 162-game season (since average is .500). - Converting that to runs uses runs-per-win. How it becomes “replacement runs” for a player: - For position players, replacement runs are usually proportional to playing time (PA). - FanGraphs uses something close to ~20 runs per 600 PA (order of magnitude; the exact constant is derived from the replacement-level winning percentage and the current run environment). - Baseball-Reference similarly uses a playing-time-based replacement run estimate, often around ~20 runs per 600 PA, with slight differences in constants and scaling. Mechanically: 1) Decide replacement level in wins below average per full season (or per PA). 2) Convert wins to runs using runs-per-win. 3) Allocate those runs across playing time. So replacement runs are a positive addition when you’re converting “above average” components into “above replacement.” If a player is exactly league average in batting, fielding, baserunning, etc., they still get positive WAR because being average is better than replacement.
So if a league-average player has 650 PA, they might still be like 2 WAR just from replacement runs?

Roughly, yes—though the exact number depends on the replacement-level constant and runs-per-win. If replacement runs are ~20 runs per 600 PA, then for 650 PA: Rrep ≈ 20 * (650/600) ≈ 21.7 runs If runs per win is ~10: WAR from replacement alone ≈ 21.7 / 10 ≈ 2.17 WAR That’s why “0 WAR” corresponds to replacement level, not average. An average full-time player is typically around 2 WAR, give or take, which aligns with how teams value everyday regulars.
