How do positional adjustments work? I hear numbers like +12.5 for shortstop or -17.5 for first base. Where do those come from?

Positional adjustments aim to account for the defensive spectrum: some positions are inherently harder and require scarcer skills (SS, C, CF), while others are easier (1B, DH). If you only used observed fielding metrics, you might undervalue players who can competently play difficult positions because their “true value” includes the fact that not everyone can play there. How the values are set: - They’re generally derived empirically by looking at offensive performance by position and/or the defensive difficulty implied by how players move between positions and how their defense changes. - The idea: if shortstops, on average, hit worse than first basemen, that implies shortstop defense is harder/more valuable, so you add runs for playing SS and subtract for playing 1B to put everyone on a common scale. Typical FanGraphs positional adjustment values per 1,350 innings (approx full season in field): - C: +12.5 (sometimes higher in some systems) - SS: +7.5 - 2B: +2.5 - 3B: +2.5 - CF: +2.5 - LF/RF: -7.5 - 1B: -12.5 - DH: -17.5 Baseball-Reference uses a similar set, with modest differences and sometimes different catcher handling. Application: Positional runs = (innings at position / 1350) * positional_value If a player splits time across positions, you weight accordingly.
Catcher framing: does WAR include it? If not, isn’t that a huge omission?

It depends on the version and era. - Historically, both major public WARs struggled to fully include catcher framing because reliable pitch-by-pitch framing metrics emerged later. - FanGraphs introduced catcher framing runs (from their framing model) and incorporated it into catcher defense for some periods, but the exact integration has changed over time and may differ between “WAR” display and separate “Framing” columns depending on season. - Baseball-Reference has also incorporated catcher defense components, but framing integration has been more limited/indirect in some versions. In principle, an ideal WAR would include: - Receiving/framing - Blocking - Throwing (controlling running game) - Fielding bunts/pops - Game-calling (hard to measure) Framing can be worth multiple runs (even 10+ runs) per season for elite framers, so excluding it would materially mis-rank catchers. Modern implementations try to include it, but you should always check what the specific WAR version includes for a given year.
On defense, you said FanGraphs uses UZR and Baseball-Reference uses DRS. What’s the difference methodologically, and why do they disagree?

UZR and DRS both aim to measure how many runs a defender saves compared to average, but they differ in data sources, zone definitions, modeling choices, and included components. UZR (Ultimate Zone Rating): - Uses batted-ball location data (historically from BIS; later Statcast-era alternatives exist). - Divides field into zones; evaluates how often a player converts balls in those zones into outs relative to average for that type/location. - Converts out probabilities and extra bases allowed into runs via linear weights. - Has components: range runs, outfield arm runs, double-play runs (infield), error runs. DRS (Defensive Runs Saved): - Also uses detailed batted-ball data (often BIS-based). - Uses a system of “plus/minus” plays: compares how often a fielder makes plays on balls with similar characteristics. - Includes additional components beyond pure range/arm, like: - Infield double-play ability - Outfield arm (holding runners, assists) - Bunt defense - Catcher throwing and, in some presentations, pitch blocking/framing components (depending on era/source) - Converts to runs saved. Why disagreement happens: - Small samples: defense is noisy year-to-year; a few plays swing run totals. - Different classification of batted balls: where exactly a ball is “located,” its speed, hang time, and how it’s bucketed can differ. - Different treatment of shifts and positioning: modern positioning complicates “responsibility.” - Different run values: how much a single vs double vs out is worth in that context-neutral framework. - Park effects and scoring: some parks create unusual batted-ball distributions. Over multiple seasons, UZR and DRS tend to correlate better, but for a single season they can diverge a lot.
How does Statcast OAA relate? If OAA is better, why not just use it in WAR?

Statcast Outs Above Average (OAA) is based on tracking data: it models the probability of making an out given the fielder’s starting position, reaction, route, sprint speed, and batted-ball characteristics (exit velocity, launch angle, etc.). Conceptually, it can capture things zone-based systems approximate. Why it’s not universally used in WAR (or why integration is gradual): - Coverage and comparability: OAA is robust in the Statcast era, but WAR needs historical continuity across decades. - Translating to runs: OAA is outs-based; converting outs to runs requires mapping the value of those outs (and the value of preventing extra bases), which is doable but requires additional modeling. - Positioning credit: if a team positions a fielder well, should the fielder get credit? Statcast attempts to account for starting position, but team positioning is part of the play outcome. - Components beyond range: arm value and preventing extra bases are handled differently; Statcast has separate metrics for arm, etc. - Public-war inertia: established WAR versions have long track records; changing defense inputs can create discontinuities. Some newer WAR presentations and research versions do incorporate Statcast-based defense (including “Fielding Run Value” frameworks), but the mainstream fWAR/bWAR defaults have historically relied on UZR/DRS (with evolving updates).
Okay, switch to pitchers. Explain fWAR for pitchers in run components. Start from FIP.

FanGraphs pitcher WAR is built around FIP (Fielding Independent Pitching), which estimates a pitcher’s run prevention based on outcomes mostly under the pitcher’s control: strikeouts, walks, hit-by-pitches, and home runs. Core steps: 1) Compute FIP: FIP = (13*HR + 3*(BB + HBP) - 2*K) / IP + constant - The constant is chosen so that league-average FIP matches league-average ERA for that season (or matches a run scale). - Sometimes IBB are excluded depending on definition; FanGraphs typically uses BB-IBB. 2) Convert FIP (a runs-per-9-ish rate) into runs allowed above/below average over innings pitched: Pitching runs above average ≈ (lgFIP - pitcherFIP) * (IP/9) Because lower FIP is better. If pitcherFIP < lgFIP, they’re positive runs. 3) Add replacement level: Replacement level for pitchers is set below average—often around a .380-ish winning percentage equivalent for pitchers, or a runs-per-9 offset from league average. FanGraphs publishes a replacement level in terms of runs per 9 (or FIP) above league average, then scales by innings. So: Runs above replacement = Runs above average + Replacement runs (positive) 4) Convert runs to wins: WAR = (Runs above replacement) / (runs per win) There are also starter vs reliever nuances: - Relievers often get a leverage adjustment in some WAR variants or are treated with different replacement levels because reliever innings are easier to replace. - FanGraphs has “RAR” and “WAR” for pitchers, and historically used a “leverage index” adjustment for relievers in some displays, though the standard pitcher WAR is mostly context-neutral.
But bWAR for pitchers is RA9-based, right? Explain that pipeline and how it accounts for defense.

Baseball-Reference pitcher WAR starts from actual runs allowed (RA9), adjusted for park and (in their approach) team defense. A simplified pipeline: 1) Start with pitcher’s runs allowed per 9 innings (RA9), usually based on total runs (earned + unearned) allowed while the pitcher was in the game, scaled per 9. 2) Adjust for park: Compute a park-adjusted RA9 to put pitchers in different parks on comparable footing. 3) Adjust for league: Account for league run environment. 4) Account for defensive support: bWAR uses a “defense behind the pitcher” adjustment. Conceptually, if a pitcher had a great defense converting balls in play into outs, their RA9 looks better than their “pitching contribution” alone. Conversely, poor defense inflates RA9. Baseball-Reference uses team defensive runs (from their defensive system) to estimate how many runs were saved/allowed by fielders and distributes that to pitchers based on balls in play or innings. The exact allocation details are complex, but the goal is to isolate pitcher value from team defense. 5) Convert to runs above average and then above replacement: As with other WAR systems, you calculate how many runs better than average the pitcher was over his innings, then add replacement runs. 6) Convert to wins using runs-per-win. Key contrast: - fWAR asks: “Given K/BB/HR, what should we expect?” (predictive-ish, defense-independent) - bWAR asks: “Given actual runs allowed, adjusted for context and defense, how many runs did he prevent?” (descriptive, results-based)
If bWAR adjusts for defense, doesn’t that make it “fielding independent” too? Why would it still differ from FIP-based?

Even with a defense adjustment, bWAR remains anchored to observed run outcomes on balls in play, sequencing, and some elements FIP explicitly ignores. Reasons differences persist: - Balls in play quality: Some pitchers may induce weak contact (or allow hard contact) in a way not captured by FIP. RA9-based WAR will reflect it; FIP-based won’t. - Sequencing (LOB%): RA9 reflects timing of hits/walks; FIP is more context-neutral. A pitcher with the same HR/BB/K can have different RA9 due to clustering of events. - Defensive adjustment is imperfect: It’s hard to allocate team defense precisely to individual pitchers, especially with shifts, catcher effects, and pitcher-specific contact profiles. - Unearned runs: RA9 includes them; FIP does not. Some unearned runs are pitcher-driven (e.g., inducing errors with hard-to-handle contact), some are not. - Park factor methods differ. - Role/leverage: Some systems treat relievers differently. So bWAR can differ because it’s closer to “what happened” after adjustments, while fWAR is “what the pitcher controlled” per the FIP model.
How do runs-per-win conversions work? You said about 9–10. Is there a formula?

A common approach is to estimate the marginal runs that correspond to one additional win in the current run environment. There are a few methods: 1) Rule-of-thumb linear approximation: Runs per win (RPW) ≈ 10 in a typical modern MLB environment. But this varies: in low-scoring eras, fewer runs correspond to a win; in high-scoring eras, more runs. 2) Empirical slope from Pythagorean expectation: If you model winning percentage as: Win% ≈ RS^γ / (RS^γ + RA^γ) Then the derivative around .500 gives a relationship between run differential and wins. This yields a runs-per-win that depends on runs scored/allowed levels. 3) Tangent method at league average: Using average runs per game (R/G), you can approximate: RPW ≈ (constant) * (R/G) You’ll see approximations like RPW ≈ 9.1 to 10.5 depending on R/G. Public WAR providers compute RPW each season based on league scoring. FanGraphs publishes “runs per win” constants used in WAR calculations.
I want to reconcile a full position-player WAR example. Give me a hypothetical player with specific batting, baserunning, fielding, positional, replacement runs; then compute WAR.

Here’s a concrete example (all in runs): Assume: - Batting (wRAA): +26 - Baserunning (BsR): +3 - Fielding (UZR or DRS): +8 - Positional adjustment: +2 (e.g., 2B/3B/CF-ish partial season) - League adjustment: +1 (small) - Replacement runs: +22 (650 PA) Total Runs Above Replacement (RAR): RAR = 26 + 3 + 8 + 2 + 1 + 22 = 62 runs Assume runs per win (RPW) = 10.0 WAR = 62 / 10.0 = 6.2 WAR That’s an MVP-caliber season. If the same player had -10 fielding instead of +8, everything else equal: RAR = 44 WAR = 4.4 WAR Same bat, but defense swings the total a lot.
Now explain why two WARs can differ even if they used the same defensive metric. Like, purely methodological differences.

Even holding the defensive input constant, differences can arise from: - Offensive run estimator differences: - FanGraphs uses wOBA-based wRAA; Baseball-Reference uses a linear-weights batting runs formulation based on event values. They’re very close conceptually, but differ in: - exact weights used (year-specific), - treatment of IBB, - treatment of reaching on error, catcher interference, - baserunning and double play components allocation. - Playing time denominators: - How PA is defined for scaling replacement runs and batting runs. - How innings are counted across positions for positional adjustment. - Park factors: - Different park factor methodologies (multi-year averaging windows, component park factors for singles/doubles/HR, etc.). - Whether park factors are applied to offense, defense, or both. - League adjustments: - Different handling of interleague play, DH penalty/bonus, and league quality in historical eras. - Replacement level: - Slightly different baseline (e.g., replacement as -20 runs per 600 PA vs -19; pitcher replacement FIP offset differs too). - Different distributions across positions (some systems might assume replacement differs by position). - Runs-per-win: - Different RPW constants (or computed differently). All of these can create differences of a few tenths to a full win, even if the “big pieces” look aligned.
Let’s talk about park factors in more detail. How do they compute them, and how do they apply them to WAR?

Park factors estimate how a ballpark affects run scoring (or specific events) relative to a neutral environment, controlling for team quality as much as possible. Computation approaches: 1) Simple runs-based park factor: PF = (Runs scored at home + runs allowed at home) / (Runs scored on road + runs allowed on road), adjusted for number of games. Often scaled so 100 is neutral: PF_index = 100 * PF 2) Multi-year stabilization: Because a single season is noisy, providers often use multi-year averages (e.g., 3-year regressed) and may weight recent years more. 3) Component park factors: Instead of just total runs, compute PFs for: - singles, doubles, triples, HR - BB, K - BABIP-ish effects This helps better adjust hitters/pitchers whose skill set interacts with the park (e.g., HR-friendly parks). Application in WAR: - For offense: convert a player’s batting line into park-adjusted runs (or adjust wOBA/wRAA inputs). FanGraphs has park-adjusted wOBA and wRC+, and WAR uses park-adjusted batting runs so players aren’t penalized for playing in a pitcher’s park (or inflated in a hitter’s park). - For pitchers: adjust their FIP or RA9 for park (especially HR rates and overall run scoring). - For defense: park effects are harder; some systems don’t park-adjust fielding explicitly beyond what’s implicit in league context. Others may incorporate park in the defensive model calibration.
Does WAR measure “value” or “talent”? I’m trying to interpret a single-season WAR.

WAR is primarily a measure of value (what happened or what was produced) in that season, not pure underlying talent. - Position-player WAR is descriptive: it sums observed batting/baserunning/fielding contributions (with context-neutral weights). - Pitcher WAR depends: - fWAR (FIP-based) is closer to an estimate of underlying performance based on defense-independent outcomes, often more predictive than RA9. - bWAR (RA9-based) is more descriptive of actual run prevention. Single-season WAR contains noise, especially defense and sequencing for pitchers. For talent evaluation, analysts often: - use multi-year WAR, - regress components (especially defense), - use underlying metrics (Statcast contact quality, K-BB%, etc.).
How does WAR handle clutch or leverage? If a player is great in high-leverage spots, does WAR capture that?

Standard WAR is designed to be context-neutral: it values events the same regardless of game situation. A home run in a blowout counts the same as a home run in the 9th inning of a tie game. So: - “Clutch” performance (timing) is not directly added. - High-leverage relief pitching is usually not fully credited beyond the value of preventing runs in general, though some versions have leverage adjustments or alternative WAR variants: - FanGraphs has “WPA” (Win Probability Added) which is leverage-based, and “WPA/LI” which removes leverage. - Some WAR displays for relievers include a leverage multiplier, but the standard pitcher WAR is typically context-neutral. The rationale: context-neutral metrics are better for comparing players independent of team context and are more stable/predictive.
I’ve heard criticism that WAR is “one number” and hides uncertainty. Can we quantify uncertainty in WAR?

You can, but public WAR typically doesn’t publish formal confidence intervals. Still, you can reason about uncertainty by component: - Batting runs: relatively stable with large PA; the uncertainty shrinks as PA grows. - Baserunning: moderate; fewer events than hitting but still measurable. - Fielding: high uncertainty year-to-year; you often want 2–3 years for a clearer signal. - Positional adjustment: deterministic given innings; low uncertainty. - Replacement runs: deterministic given playing time; low uncertainty. - Pitching: - FIP components stabilize at different rates (K fastest, HR slower). - RA9 is noisy due to BABIP, sequencing, and defense. Analysts sometimes use heuristic error bars: - Position-player WAR: ±1 win (or more) for single season depending on defensive reliance. - Pitcher WAR: can be ±1+ win, especially for relievers with low innings. A more formal approach: treat each component as an estimate with variance, assume some correlations, and propagate variance to WAR. But it requires assumptions and data.
Can you explain the relationship between WAR and wRC+? People say “wRC+ is rate, WAR is counting.” How do they connect?

wRC+ is a rate statistic: it measures a hitter’s runs created per plate appearance relative to league average, adjusted for park, scaled so 100 is average. WAR uses a counting measure of offensive runs: - FanGraphs uses wRAA (a counting stat) derived from wOBA and PA. - wRC+ is closely related to wRAA but normalized per PA and scaled. Connection: - If you know a player’s wRC+ and PA, you can approximate their batting runs above average by converting the rate advantage into runs. But you need the league run scale and conversion constants (wOBA scale / runs per PA). Conceptually: wRC+ tells you “how good per opportunity.” WAR’s batting component tells you “how many total runs did he add with the bat,” which is rate * opportunities. So a 150 wRC+ in 650 PA produces a lot more batting runs than 150 wRC+ in 300 PA.
What about comparing players across eras? If WAR is in wins, shouldn’t it be comparable?

WAR is designed to be broadly comparable across eras because: - It’s relative to league average and replacement within that season. - It uses season-specific run environments (wOBA weights, FIP constants, runs-per-win). - It includes positional adjustments that are intended to be stable. But perfect comparability is hard: - Data quality: defensive data is much better in recent decades; older eras rely on less precise estimates. - Role changes: pitcher usage, reliever specialization, innings loads, and substitution patterns differ. - League quality and integration: talent pool changes, expansion, segregation eras, etc. - Changes in equipment, parks, travel, and strategy. So WAR is a strong starting point for cross-era comparisons, but you should interpret with historical context and recognize methodological uncertainty increases the further back you go.
Let’s do a deeper dive on batting runs for Baseball-Reference. How is Rbat computed? Is it basically linear weights from events?

Yes, Rbat is essentially linear weights: assign each batting event a run value (relative to an out) based on how much that event increases expected runs in a context-neutral average base/out state distribution. A simplified linear weights model: Batting Runs = Σ (value(event) * count(event)) + constant_adjustment Events include: - 1B, 2B, 3B, HR - BB, HBP - Outs (including different out types sometimes) - Sometimes SB/CS are separated into baserunning rather than batting. The “run values” are derived from run expectancy tables for that season or an averaged environment. They reflect that: - A HR is worth more than a 1B because it always scores at least one run and clears bases. - A BB is worth less than a 1B because it advances runners less and doesn’t guarantee an extra base. - Making an out has negative value relative to average. Baseball-Reference then compares the player’s linear weights runs to league average given the same outs/PA to get “above average” runs. FanGraphs wOBA/wRAA is also a linear-weights system, just compressed into a rate (wOBA) and then expanded back to runs (wRAA) with wOBA scale. So the two are close; differences are implementation details.
How do stolen bases get valued in BsR? Is it just (SB * run_value) - (CS * run_cost)?

That’s the core idea, but BsR is usually a sum of multiple baserunning subcomponents. FanGraphs baserunning: - wSB: stolen base runs wSB = (SB * run_value_SB) + (CS * run_value_CS) + (pickoffs etc) The run values are derived from run expectancy: a successful steal increases expected runs; a caught stealing decreases it more (because you lose a runner and an out). - UBR: “ultimate baserunning” for non-SB baserunning Captures taking extra bases on hits (1st-to-3rd on single, scoring from 2nd, tagging up), avoiding outs, etc. - Sometimes includes wGDP or other running-related adjustments depending on presentation. So BsR = wSB + UBR + (other small components) The valuation depends on base/out state averages, not game leverage.
If a player hits into a lot of double plays, where does that show up in WAR?

It depends on the WAR flavor. - Baseball-Reference explicitly tracks “Rdp” (runs from grounding into double plays relative to average). That then contributes to the overall runs above average. - FanGraphs historically included GIDP effects within its offensive linear weights or as part of baserunning/UBR-like adjustments depending on the era and data. In many public displays, FanGraphs splits out “wGDP” separately and/or includes it in BsR or batting runs depending on the version. Conceptually, hitting into a double play is worse than a normal out because it removes a baserunner and adds an extra out; linear weights assign a more negative value to GIDP events.
How does WAR treat players who play multiple positions? Suppose someone plays 700 innings at SS and 600 at 2B.

Positional adjustment is weighted by innings at each position. If SS adjustment is +7.5 runs per 1350 innings, and 2B is +2.5 per 1350: PosRuns = (700/1350)*7.5 + (600/1350)*2.5 = (0.5185)*7.5 + (0.4444)*2.5 = 3.89 + 1.11 = 5.00 runs (approx) Then that positional runs value is included in the RAR sum. Defense itself (UZR/DRS) is also position-specific and would be computed separately for SS and 2B, then summed.
What about DH? If a player is mostly DH, is the positional adjustment the only penalty?

In WAR frameworks, the DH penalty is primarily via positional adjustment (e.g., -17.5 runs per 1350 innings), reflecting that DH provides no defensive value and is easier to fill. However, there are a few nuances: - Some systems treat DH as a “position” with its own replacement baseline; but in public WAR, it’s effectively handled by the positional runs penalty. - Because DH has no fielding component, the player’s WAR is driven mostly by batting and baserunning plus replacement runs, minus the DH positional penalty. - If a DH is a great hitter, they can still be very valuable; they just need to hit enough to offset the positional penalty.
Now, can you explain why pitcher WAR treats relievers differently? I’ve seen elite closers with fewer WAR than starters even if their ERAs are amazing.

Two main reasons: 1) Inning volume: WAR is cumulative value. Starters throw 180–220 innings; relievers might throw 60–75. Even with a much better rate (ERA/FIP), the total runs prevented can be smaller. 2) Replacement level and role: Reliever innings are generally easier to replace than starter innings. Replacement level for relievers is sometimes set differently. Also: - fWAR uses FIP; many elite relievers allow few runs partly via sequencing and contact management; if their K/BB/HR profile isn’t as extreme, FIP might be higher than ERA. - bWAR uses RA9; it may credit the low runs allowed, but still the limited innings caps RAR. Leverage: Closers pitch in high leverage; WPA captures that. WAR usually doesn’t fully add leverage, so a closer’s “win impact” in a narrative sense can exceed their context-neutral WAR.
If I wanted a leverage-adjusted WAR, what would I do? Is it as simple as multiplying by LI?

A crude approach is to apply a leverage index (LI) multiplier to runs prevented in relief innings, but it’s not that simple if you want consistency: - WPA is already leverage-weighted but includes sequencing and context; it’s not a replacement-level framework. - FanGraphs sometimes reports “WPA/LI,” which removes leverage effects; it’s closer to context-neutral value. - A leverage-adjusted WAR would need: 1) a baseline for replacement relievers in similar leverage usage, 2) a method to allocate leverage (managers choose when to use pitchers), 3) avoid double-counting context. Some analysts compute: “gmLI-WAR” variants where the pitcher’s run prevention is scaled by average leverage index of their appearances. But it becomes partly a measure of managerial usage and role, not just skill. So yes, LI can be used, but you have to be explicit about what question you’re answering: “value in role as used” vs “value independent of usage.”
Back to fWAR vs bWAR for position players: can you list the most common causes of big discrepancies for a single player-season?

The largest single driver is usually defense. Common causes: 1) Defensive metric disagreement: - UZR vs DRS can differ by 10–20 runs in a season for the same player, which is 1–2 WAR. 2) Position/positional adjustment differences: - If a player splits time between positions and the sites classify innings differently (or handle position eligibility differently), positional runs can differ by several runs. 3) Park factor differences: - Especially in unusual parks or seasons, batting runs after park adjustment can differ. 4) Baserunning component differences: - Differences in how extra bases taken, pickoffs, and double plays are valued can add/subtract a few runs. 5) Playing time accounting: - Slight differences in PA or innings used for scaling replacement. But defense is the headline: a 15-run difference in fielding is ~1.5 WAR if RPW is 10.
Can we do an explicit discrepancy example? Hypothetical player where offense is identical, but defense differs between UZR and DRS.

Sure. Assume both systems agree on: - Batting runs: +20 - Baserunning: +2 - Positional: +5 - League: +1 - Replacement: +20 Total non-defense runs = 48 Now defense: - UZR says +12 - DRS says -5 FanGraphs-like RAR: RAR_f = 48 + 12 = 60 WAR_f = 60 / 10 = 6.0 Baseball-Reference-like RAR: RAR_b = 48 + (-5) = 43 WAR_b = 43 / 10 = 4.3 Same hitter, same playing time, but 1.7 WAR difference driven purely by defense estimate.
I’m also confused about “above average” vs “above replacement.” Why not just use above average? Wouldn’t that be simpler?

Above average is simpler, and many components are naturally computed that way (wRAA, defensive runs vs average). But above replacement is more useful for roster construction and “value”: - Teams don’t choose between “player” and “average player” on the margin. They choose between “player” and “who we can get for cheap if we don’t roster him.” - Replacement level represents the opportunity cost of a roster spot. - WAR answers: “How many wins did this player add compared to a freely available alternative?” Above-average metrics are still valuable: - They isolate quality independent of playing time baseline. - They’re good for comparing rate performance. But WAR’s replacement baseline makes it additive across a roster: total team WAR + replacement-level team wins ≈ team wins (approximately).
Wait, can team WAR really add up to team wins?

Approximately, with caveats. In principle: Team Wins ≈ Replacement Wins + Team WAR If replacement-level winning percentage is around .294, replacement wins over 162 games is: 162 * 0.294 ≈ 47.6 wins So if a team totals 40 WAR: Expected wins ≈ 47.6 + 40 ≈ 87.6 wins In practice, differences arise because: - WAR is context-neutral and uses approximations. - Pitcher and position-player WAR can overlap or leave gaps depending on how defense is allocated between pitchers and fielders. - Bullpen sequencing and leverage can cause actual wins to deviate. - Run-to-win conversion is approximate. But as a rough accounting identity, it often works reasonably well.

How do they avoid double counting defense between pitchers and fielders? If pitcher WAR is RA9-based with defense adjustment, and position-player WAR includes fielding runs, aren’t they counting the same runs twice?

This is a real accounting challenge. - In a fully consistent framework, you’d want the sum of pitcher WAR + position-player WAR to map to team runs allowed and runs scored without double counting. - bWAR tries to allocate defensive value to fielders (via DRS) and then adjust pitcher runs allowed for defensive support so that pitcher value reflects pitching, not fielding. That helps reduce double counting. - fWAR uses FIP for pitchers, which largely sidesteps balls-in-play defense: pitchers are credited for K/BB/HR, and fielders get credit for converting balls in play via UZR. Since FIP ignores balls in play, you’re less likely to double count fielding with pitcher value, but you can create a “gap” between FIP-based pitcher value and actual runs allowed by the team (because contact quality, sequencing, etc. aren’t assigned). So: - RA9-based pitching WAR needs explicit defense adjustments to avoid overlap. - FIP-based pitching WAR avoids overlap but doesn’t “explain” all runs allowed. Neither is perfect; they’re different decompositions of the same reality.
Is there a way to convert between fWAR and bWAR? Like a mapping or regression?

There’s no exact conversion because they measure different things and use different inputs. But you can build an empirical mapping: - For a large sample of player-seasons, regress bWAR on fWAR (or vice versa) and include covariates like: - defensive position, - innings/PA, - defensive metric differences (UZR vs DRS), - for pitchers: (ERA - FIP), BABIP, HR/FB, LOB%. You’d get an average relationship (slope near 1, intercept near 0), but with substantial residuals for: - defense-heavy players, - pitchers with unusual sequencing/contact outcomes, - catchers (framing treatment differences). So you can approximate, but you can’t “convert” a specific season with precision without re-computing components under one framework.
Let’s talk about the FIP constant. How is it determined exactly?

The FIP constant is chosen so that league-average FIP equals league-average ERA (or sometimes equals league-average RA9 on an earned-run scale) for that season. Procedure: 1) Compute the “raw” FIP numerator per IP for all pitchers in the league: rawFIP = (13*HR + 3*(BB + HBP) - 2*K) / IP 2) Compute league-average ERA: lgERA = (ER * 9) / IP 3) Set constant = lgERA - (rawFIP * 9?) depending on whether rawFIP is per inning or already per 9. In the typical formula: FIP = ((13*HR + 3*(BB + HBP) - 2*K) / IP) + constant That yields a “per inning” term plus constant; but most presentations treat FIP as ERA-like per 9. Many implementations incorporate the *9 into the numerator/denominator so units align. The key point: constant aligns the scale so FIP and ERA share the same league average. Thus, FIP is comparable to ERA in magnitude, but built from different components.
You mentioned that FanGraphs uses BB-IBB. Why exclude intentional walks?

Intentional walks are often excluded because: - They’re heavily context-driven (game situation, hitter quality, base open). - They may reflect managerial strategy more than pitcher skill. - Including them can distort evaluation, especially for pitchers facing Barry Bonds-like hitters in certain eras. So using BB-IBB aims to better capture the pitcher’s “true” walk skill rather than tactical IBB.
For hitters, are intentional walks treated differently in wOBA/wRAA?

Often, yes. Many versions of wOBA exclude intentional walks from the denominator and/or treat them differently because: - IBB are not purely a result of hitter skill; they’re strategic. - Including them can inflate value for hitters who are intentionally walked a lot, which might be partially deserved (fear factor) but is also context-dependent. That said, there’s debate: elite hitters “earn” IBB by being dangerous. Public metrics choose a convention for consistency and predictive utility. FanGraphs’ published wOBA formula typically excludes IBB from the wOBA denominator.
I want to understand the linear weights underlying wOBA. How are the weights chosen?

wOBA weights are derived from empirical run values of events in that season’s run environment. Method sketch: 1) Build a run expectancy matrix: expected runs scored from each base-out state (24 states: 0/1/2 outs × 8 base states). 2) For each event (BB, HBP, 1B, 2B, 3B, HR), compute the average change in run expectancy plus runs scored on the play across all occurrences. This yields an average run value per event. 3) Normalize the weights so that wOBA is on an OBP-like scale: - Choose a scaling factor so league wOBA ≈ league OBP (historically), or so the metric has convenient magnitude. 4) Publish yearly weights because run values change with run environment (e.g., HR more/less valuable depending on scoring, base/out distribution). So weights aren’t arbitrary; they’re derived from observed run impacts.
If wOBA is already based on run values, why do we need wOBA_scale to convert to runs?

Because wOBA is a rate on a convenient scale, not directly “runs per PA.” The event weights in wOBA are normalized so the resulting number looks like OBP (~.320–.340 league average). That normalization compresses/expands the raw run values. wOBA_scale reverses that normalization: it converts differences in wOBA into differences in runs per plate appearance. So: - wOBA: convenient rate scale - wOBA_scale: mapping from wOBA points to runs/PA - wRAA: total runs above average = (wOBA difference / scale) * PA
How does WAR handle partial seasons or players with few PA? Does it break?

It doesn’t break, but it becomes less reliable. Mechanically, WAR scales linearly with playing time: - Batting runs scale with PA - Replacement runs scale with PA - Positional adjustment scales with innings - Defense metrics can be noisy in small samples, and some systems regress or limit defensive credit in tiny samples, but public WAR often reports it anyway. So a bench player with 150 PA could show, say, 1.5 WAR if they had extreme performance and/or very good defense in limited innings. But uncertainty is huge, especially on defense. For evaluation, you’d want to consider sample size and regress to mean.
I want you to address a common complaint: “WAR is just made up because you can’t compare a shortstop to a first baseman.” How does WAR justify cross-position comparison?

WAR’s cross-position comparison rests on two pillars: 1) Runs are a common currency: Everything is translated into runs above average (batting runs, baserunning runs, fielding runs). Runs are additive: a run saved on defense is worth about the same as a run created on offense in a context-neutral framework. 2) Positional adjustment: Because positions differ in difficulty and scarcity, WAR adds/subtracts a run value to reflect the average defensive value of merely being able to play that position at MLB level. This allows comparison: - A +5 defensive SS who hits like a league-average SS might be more valuable than a 1B with similar hitting, because the SS provides value at a scarcer position. - Conversely, a 1B has to hit more to be equally valuable. It’s not perfect, but it’s a principled way to put players on the same scale.
Let’s talk about “replacement level by position.” Is replacement level the same for all positions? It seems like catcher replacement is worse than first base replacement.

Public WAR typically uses a mostly uniform replacement level in terms of runs per PA (for position players), then uses positional adjustments to capture scarcity. That effectively creates position-specific replacement baselines. How it works: - If you give everyone the same replacement runs per PA, then: - A catcher gets +12.5 positional runs (per full season), which raises their baseline value relative to a DH. - A DH gets -17.5, which lowers it. So even with uniform replacement, the overall “replacement baseline” by position differs once you include positional adjustment. Some analysts argue for explicit position-specific replacement levels, but the combination of uniform replacement + positional adjustments already approximates the notion that it’s harder to find a competent catcher/shortstop than a 1B/DH.
How do they handle pitchers batting (in pre-DH or NL pre-universal DH)? Does pitcher hitting count in WAR?

Typically, pitcher hitting is treated within position-player WAR for pitchers as hitters, but it’s often presented separately or folded into pitcher WAR in some historical presentations. - Baseball-Reference historically included pitcher batting runs in pitcher WAR totals (or at least accounted for it in some way for total value), because pitchers did hit and contribute (usually negatively). - FanGraphs has had separate batting WAR for pitchers as hitters in some displays. In the universal DH era, this mostly disappears. In earlier eras, pitcher hitting could matter by a win or more for good-hitting pitchers (rare) or for teams with exceptionally poor pitcher hitting, but usually it’s a small negative.
If I want to use WAR for forecasting, which version is better?

For forecasting: - For hitters, either is fine; the differences are mostly defense. You’d likely want to: - regress defensive component heavily, - use multi-year averages, - incorporate aging curves and playing time projections. - For pitchers: - fWAR (FIP-based) is often more predictive of future ERA than RA9-based metrics because it focuses on K/BB/HR, which are more stable. - But modern forecasting also uses Statcast (xERA, xwOBA allowed), K-BB%, and other contact-quality measures. So many forecasters start from defense-independent pitching metrics (FIP, xFIP, SIERA) rather than RA9-based WAR.
You said xFIP and SIERA. How do those relate to WAR? Are there WAR versions based on them?

xFIP and SIERA are alternative pitcher run estimators: - xFIP replaces a pitcher’s actual HR with an expected HR based on fly balls and league-average HR/FB rate: xFIP = (13*expectedHR + 3*(BB + HBP) - 2*K) / IP + constant This reduces noise from HR/FB variance. - SIERA models run prevention using K, BB, and batted-ball types (GB/FB), with interactions, aiming to better capture pitcher skill. WAR is typically anchored to either FIP or RA9 in mainstream public versions. But in principle you can build a WAR-like metric from any run estimator: 1) Convert estimator to runs over innings relative to average 2) Add replacement runs 3) Divide by runs per win Some research and private team models do this, but public “WAR” branding is mostly fWAR (FIP) and bWAR (RA9).
There’s also WARP from Baseball Prospectus. How does that fit in? Why is it different again?

WARP (Wins Above Replacement Player) is Baseball Prospectus’ WAR-like framework. It differs because: - It uses its own defensive metrics (e.g., FRAA / DRA-based systems depending on era). - It uses different batting runs calculations and replacement level assumptions. - For pitchers, BP often uses DRA (Deserved Run Average) concepts to isolate pitcher contribution given context, framing, defense, etc. So WARP can diverge meaningfully from fWAR/bWAR, especially for catchers (framing) and pitchers (context adjustments).
I want to be careful: when someone says “WAR,” should I always ask which one?

Yes, because “WAR” is a family of metrics, not a single standardized formula. Best practice: - Specify: fWAR (FanGraphs), bWAR (Baseball-Reference), WARP (Baseball Prospectus). - For pitchers, clarify whether it’s FIP-based or RA9-based. - For catchers, check framing inclusion. When comparing players, use the same WAR version to avoid mixing frameworks.
Let’s do a technical check: Suppose a pitcher has 200 IP, league FIP 4.20, pitcher FIP 3.20. What are his runs above average from FIP?

Runs above average (RAA) from FIP, using the rate difference: RAA ≈ (lgFIP - pFIP) * (IP/9) = (4.20 - 3.20) * (200/9) = 1.00 * 22.222... ≈ 22.2 runs above average Then you’d add replacement runs (maybe ~20 runs per 200 IP depending on constants) to get runs above replacement, then divide by runs per win.
What’s a typical replacement run value for pitchers per inning?

Order of magnitude: replacement level might be around 0.38 wins% which corresponds to being about 0.62 wins below .500 per game; translating to runs depends on RPW. Practically, FanGraphs often sets pitcher replacement level as about 0.5 to 0.7 runs per 9 worse than league average (in FIP terms) for starters, with relievers treated differently. If replacement is, say, 0.6 runs/9 worse than average, then replacement runs over 200 IP: Rrep ≈ 0.6 * (200/9) ≈ 13.3 runs Some seasons and implementations use larger offsets; the point is: it’s computed from a per-9 replacement gap times innings. If you want exact, you’d use the published FanGraphs replacement level constant for that season.
So with our example, if replacement is 0.6 runs/9, runs above replacement is 22.2 + 13.3 = 35.5. Then WAR ~ 3.6 if RPW is 10?
Exactly with those assumptions: WAR ≈ 35.5 / 10 = 3.55 WAR If RPW were 9.5, it’d be ~3.7. If replacement level were set at 0.8 runs/9, it’d be higher. The constants matter.
How do you compute WAR for a two-way player? Do you just add batting WAR and pitching WAR?

Generally, yes: total WAR = batting/position-player WAR + pitching WAR, with care taken to avoid double counting playing time and to treat their role properly. For a two-way player: - As a hitter: compute position-player WAR based on PA, offense, baserunning, fielding/positional runs. - As a pitcher: compute pitcher WAR based on IP and pitching run estimator. - Sum them. Caveats: - If the player’s “position” as a hitter is DH when they pitch, positional adjustments and roster/usage constraints aren’t fully captured by simple addition. - Some models might include an extra roster spot flexibility value, but public WAR generally doesn’t.
I want to go deeper on the “runs above average” baseline. For hitters, average is league average, but is that by position or overall?

For batting runs above average, the baseline is generally overall league average per PA, not position-specific. That’s intentional: the positional adjustment is what accounts for differences in typical offensive output by position. So: - Batting runs: compared to league average hitter (all positions combined). - Fielding runs: compared to average fielder at that position. - Positional adjustment: adds/subtracts to reflect position scarcity/difficulty. - Replacement runs: shifts baseline from average to replacement. This decomposition avoids mixing “position effects” into batting runs and keeps components interpretable.
For fielding runs, you said compared to average at that position. So an average shortstop gets 0 fielding runs, then +7.5 positional runs?

Correct. - Fielding runs: 0 means average defender at shortstop. - Positional adjustment: +7.5 (per full season) credits the value of playing SS at all, relative to a neutral position baseline (often 2B/3B/CF around +2.5 and 1B/LF/RF negative). - So an average defensive SS gets positive positional value. That’s why you can have a shortstop with mediocre bat but still decent WAR: the position itself carries value.
How do they choose the “zero point” position? Like why is 2B +2.5 and SS +7.5 instead of 0 and +5?

The positional adjustment system is relative; only differences matter. Providers choose a scale anchored so that: - The sum of positional adjustments across all positions, weighted by playing time distribution, roughly nets to zero (or some consistent baseline). - The adjustments reflect observed offensive scarcity and defensive difficulty. Often, 2B/3B/CF are treated as near-neutral-ish in the middle, with SS/C higher and 1B/LF/RF/DH lower. The exact zero point isn’t “true” in a physical sense; it’s a convention that makes the accounting work and keeps totals consistent.
Let’s do a check on additivity. If you sum all player WAR in the league, should it equal something?

In an idealized accounting: - Sum of all players’ “above average” runs across the league should be ~0 (because average is the mean). - Sum of all players’ “above replacement” runs should be positive because replacement is below average and everyone is above replacement by some amount on average (since playing time is filled by major leaguers, not replacement players). In practice: - The sum of WAR across MLB is roughly equal to the gap between MLB and replacement times number of games/teams, which is a large positive number. - Because replacement level is set to correspond to a certain number of wins below .500, you can estimate the total WAR in a season: Total WAR ≈ (League wins - replacement wins) across all teams. Since total league wins is fixed (each game has a win), this becomes a constant-ish per season. So the league total WAR is not arbitrary; it’s tied to the replacement level assumption.
That’s interesting. Can you put numbers on total WAR in a season?

Approximate: There are 30 teams, 162 games each, but each game involves two teams, so total games is 30*162/2 = 2430 games, thus 2430 wins in the league. If replacement winning percentage is 0.294, replacement wins per team is 47.6, so across 30 teams: Replacement wins total ≈ 47.6 * 30 ≈ 1428 wins Total WAR across the league would then be: 2430 - 1428 ≈ 1002 WAR So you’d expect total WAR in MLB to be on the order of ~1000 each season (depending on exact replacement level). That’s a useful sanity check.
Now I want to understand how WAR handles mid-season trades and league changes. Is league adjustment applied per league stint?

Typically, yes: - Offensive and pitching stats are naturally split by team/league if needed, and park factors differ by home park. - League adjustment, if applied, would be weighted by PA/IP in each league. In the modern era with universal DH and extensive interleague, league adjustments are smaller. Historically (AL with DH vs NL without, or different league quality), it mattered more. Park adjustment is definitely applied by team stint because half your games are in your home park, and if you change parks midseason, your environment changes.
How do park factors handle unbalanced schedules? Like if you play more games in your division.

Park factor computation can be biased by schedule because: - Teams play more games against certain opponents, and opponents have their own offensive/defensive quality. - If you face strong pitching staffs more often at home than on the road due to schedule quirks, naive PF estimates can be distorted. To mitigate: - Multi-year averaging reduces noise. - Some methods adjust for opponent quality by comparing how teams hit in that park vs how they hit elsewhere, and similarly for opponents. - More advanced models use regression frameworks that estimate park effects as fixed effects while controlling for team and opponent strengths. Public providers use varying degrees of sophistication; that’s one reason different sites’ park factors differ.
I’ve seen “RAR” on Baseball-Reference. How is RAR related to WAR?

RAR is “Runs Above Replacement.” WAR is just RAR divided by runs per win. So: WAR = RAR / RPW RAR is useful because: - It’s additive in runs, which can be easier to interpret for component breakdowns. - It avoids the slight variation introduced by RPW assumptions. If you know a player has 50 RAR and RPW is 10, that’s 5 WAR.
Are there cases where WAR can be negative? What does -1 WAR mean?

Yes. Negative WAR means the player performed worse than replacement level in the measured components over their playing time. Interpretation: - A -1 WAR player cost the team about one win relative to what a replacement-level fill-in would have provided over the same playing time. - This can happen with poor hitters who get a lot of PA, or defenders rated very poorly, or pitchers with very poor run prevention over many innings. Teams might still roster such players due to injuries, lack of alternatives, or because they expect improvement.
Let’s get more granular about defense. How do UZR and DRS convert plays into runs? Is it just outs times some constant?

It’s more detailed than “outs times constant” because different outs prevent different base advancements and have different run values. General approach: 1) For each batted ball, estimate the probability an average fielder at that position makes the out given location/type (and sometimes speed). 2) If the fielder makes the play when average probability is low, they get credit; if they miss a play that average fielders usually make, they get debit. 3) Translate that “out probability over/under” into runs: - Turning a hit into an out saves runs: you prevent a baserunner and an out is added. - Also consider whether the miss becomes a single vs double vs triple, and how many bases runners advance. 4) Add arm components: - Outfield arms: preventing extra bases and throwing out runners have run values. - Infield arms: less prominent; double plays matter. 5) Add error components: - Errors extend innings and create baserunners; run values account for that. So yes, outs are central, but the run conversion uses linear weights based on base/out state impacts and typical advancement patterns.
Are defensive metrics park-adjusted? Like Fenway’s LF is weird.

Park effects matter a lot for defense, but explicit park adjustment is difficult. - Some defensive systems implicitly account for park because the distribution of batted balls and play difficulty is baked into the data: if a park yields many unusual plays, the model’s average probabilities may incorporate that environment. - But if the model is trained league-wide without park-specific calibration, a fielder in a quirky park could be mis-evaluated. - Fenway’s LF (Green Monster) changes the difficulty profile: fewer “catchable” fly balls near the wall, more caroms and doubles. Arm and range responsibilities differ. Some advanced models attempt park/zone-specific baselines, but public metrics still have limitations. That’s one reason you should be cautious interpreting single-season defensive WAR in unusual parks or positions.
What about shifting? If a team shifts a lot, does the fielder get credit for making plays because of positioning?

This is a major modern issue. - Traditional zone-based metrics assumed relatively standard positioning. With shifts, a “3B” might be playing shallow RF, and responsibility boundaries blur. - If the data system assigns the play to the fielder’s listed position without accounting for starting position, it can mis-credit. - Statcast-based metrics (like OAA) use starting position and can better account for positioning. But even then, who should get credit—player or team? If a team positions well, the fielder’s probability of making the play increases; OAA compares to expected probability given that position, which tends to reduce “free credit” from being placed perfectly, but there are still questions. Public WAR versions have been updating defensive inputs to better handle this, but it’s an evolving area.
Let’s pivot to the philosophical side. What is WAR optimizing for? Is it trying to be “true talent value” or “marginal wins added”?

WAR is designed as a context-neutral estimate of marginal wins added relative to replacement, using observable components. - It’s closer to “marginal wins” than “true talent,” because it sums what the player produced in that season (or what the estimator says they produced, for pitchers in fWAR). - It’s not fully “marginal wins in context” because it strips leverage/lineup effects. - It’s not purely “true talent” because it doesn’t regress noisy components by default (publicly) and includes luck. So: it’s a value metric with a neutral context, intended to be comparable across players and additive.
If WAR is context-neutral, why does it need park factors? Isn’t park also context?

Park is context, but it’s a persistent, structural context that affects the translation from skill to observed outcomes. Context-neutral in WAR usually means: - Not dependent on teammates, lineup position, or timing of events (clutch). - Park is treated as an environmental factor you want to neutralize to compare skill/value across players on different teams. If you didn’t adjust for parks, you’d systematically overrate hitters in hitter-friendly parks and underrate those in pitcher-friendly parks, even if they had the same underlying performance. So park adjustment is part of making the metric comparable, not a violation of the intent.
How do you evaluate a player whose value is mostly defense? Like a glove-first shortstop with a 75 wRC+.

WAR is actually well-suited for that, conceptually, because it puts defense on the same run scale as offense. Example: - Batting runs might be negative (below average hitter). - But fielding runs could be strongly positive. - Add positional adjustment and replacement runs, and the player can still be 3–4 WAR if they’re elite defensively and play every day. The key is defensive metric confidence: for glove-first players, differences between UZR/DRS/OAA can swing their WAR a lot. Multi-year defensive evaluation and scouting/context help.
I want a more detailed worked example for a glove-first SS and a bat-first 1B, both with the same WAR. Show how the components could balance.

Example A: Glove-first SS (650 PA, 1350 innings at SS) - Batting runs: -10 (below average hitter) - Baserunning: +2 - Fielding runs: +20 (elite defense) - Positional: +7.5 - League: +1 - Replacement: +22 RAR = -10 + 2 + 20 + 7.5 + 1 + 22 = 42.5 WAR (RPW=10) = 4.25 Example B: Bat-first 1B (650 PA, 1350 innings at 1B) - Batting runs: +25 - Baserunning: -1 - Fielding runs: -5 (below average) - Positional: -12.5 - League: +1 - Replacement: +22 RAR = 25 - 1 - 5 - 12.5 + 1 + 22 = 29.5 WAR = 2.95 To make them equal WAR, the 1B needs more bat: If batting runs were +38 instead of +25: RAR = 38 - 1 - 5 - 12.5 + 1 + 22 = 42.5 WAR = 4.25 So the SS can “get there” via defense + position; the 1B needs a lot more offense to offset the positional penalty and weaker defense.
Great. Now, how does WAR handle corner outfield vs center field? The positional adjustment is only 10 runs difference (2.5 vs -7.5). Is that enough?

The magnitude is based on empirical scarcity and observed offensive differences by position. Whether it’s “enough” is debated, but the logic is: - CF is harder than LF/RF, but not as hard as SS/C. - Many CF can move to corner OF; fewer corner OF can move to CF at MLB level. - The adjustment is meant to reflect the average difference in defensive value/skill scarcity, not necessarily the maximum. If you think the adjustment underestimates CF difficulty, you’d see: - CF being undervalued relative to corners in WAR. But defensive metrics themselves often rate CF range and impact strongly, which also adds value beyond the positional constant. The positional adjustment is only one piece; actual fielding runs can be large for elite CF defenders.
How do they decide replacement level for pitchers vs hitters? Is it the same .294 baseline?

The .294 replacement winning percentage is often discussed at the team level. Translating that to individual hitters and pitchers requires allocating replacement runs to batting and pitching. Approaches: - For hitters: replacement runs per PA is set so that a roster of replacement players would produce replacement-level offense, given typical playing time distribution. - For pitchers: replacement runs per IP is set similarly for run prevention. The exact split between “replacement offense” and “replacement pitching/defense” depends on the WAR model. FanGraphs and Baseball-Reference have their own constants. But both are calibrated so that if you sum WAR across all players, you roughly match the league’s win total above replacement.
Are there separate replacement levels for starters and relievers?

Often, yes in practice. Reasons: - Replacement starters (spot starters, AAA call-ups) tend to be worse than replacement relievers in terms of run prevention per inning, but they throw more innings and face lineups multiple times. - The distribution of readily available talent differs by role. - Some WAR models apply different replacement baselines for starters vs relievers, or incorporate role adjustments (e.g., “starter/reliever runs”). FanGraphs historically has different replacement levels by role and uses a “reliever adjustment” or leverage-related considerations in some contexts.
How do you compute pitcher WAR for someone who both starts and relieves in the same season?

Typically: - Split their innings into starting and relieving buckets. - Apply the appropriate replacement level (and perhaps role adjustment) to each bucket. - Compute runs above average from the chosen run estimator (FIP or RA9) over total innings, but replacement runs might be role-weighted. - Convert to WAR. Some public implementations may not fully split; they might treat all innings similarly or apply a blended adjustment. The details vary.
I’d like to understand how WAR treats bullpen “inherited runners.” If a reliever allows inherited runners to score, is that in RA9? In FIP?

In RA9-based metrics: - Inherited runners scoring are charged to the pitcher who allowed them to reach base, not the reliever, under standard scoring (earned runs attribution rules are complex). But total runs allowed while the reliever is in the game can be tracked separately. - bWAR uses runs allowed attributed to the pitcher (including unearned) in standard accounting, so inherited runners are generally not charged to the reliever. In FIP-based metrics: - Inherited runners scoring don’t directly show up because FIP only counts HR, BB, HBP, K. - The reliever could allow a single that scores an inherited runner; FIP doesn’t care unless it’s a HR (or walk/HBP). This is one reason reliever evaluation is tricky. Some advanced models use “RE24” or “shutdown/meltdown” stats that account for inherited runners and leverage, but WAR’s standard pitcher accounting doesn’t fully capture that situational aspect.
So WAR might underrate firemen who strand inherited runners?

In a context-neutral sense, yes, because stranding inherited runners is partly sequencing and leverage. WAR tends to: - credit pitchers for their overall run estimator over their innings (or their attributed runs), - not fully credit specific high-leverage inherited-run situations. Metrics like WPA, RE24, and leverage-adjusted run values are better at capturing that “as used” impact, though they mix skill and context.
Let’s talk about the denominator differences: innings pitched vs batters faced. Would a better WAR use batters faced?

There’s an argument for using batters faced (TBF) because: - Pitchers are responsible for outcomes per batter, and IP is an outcome that depends on defense and sequencing (double plays, etc.). - Using TBF can reduce distortions from how outs are recorded. However: - IP is the traditional accounting unit and ties directly to runs allowed per 9. - FIP is defined per IP, and many constants are calibrated that way. - Converting everything to per TBF is doable but would require re-deriving constants and replacement levels. Some advanced models effectively evaluate per batter (e.g., DRA-like approaches), but public WAR remains IP-based for simplicity and tradition.
There’s also the issue of “pitcher responsibility” for errors. bWAR uses RA9 including unearned runs, but are unearned runs really pitcher responsibility?

Not entirely. Unearned runs can result from: - Fielding errors (fielder responsibility), - Passed balls (catcher), - Scoring decisions. But some unearned runs might still be pitcher-influenced (hard contact leading to errors, or wild pitches extending innings). The clean separation is hard. bWAR includes unearned runs to better capture actual runs allowed while acknowledging defense adjustment. That’s a choice: it leans toward describing real run prevention rather than only “earned” outcomes. fWAR avoids this by focusing on FIP components.
How should I use WAR in player comparisons then? If two players have 5 WAR each but one is defense-heavy and one is offense-heavy, are they equally valuable?

In the WAR framework, yes: 5 WAR is 5 wins above replacement. But in practice, teams might value them differently based on: - Risk/variance: defense metrics are noisier; offense tends to be more stable. - Aging curves: defense often declines earlier; some skill sets age differently. - Positional scarcity: a 5-WAR shortstop may be harder to replace than a 5-WAR first baseman even if WAR says they’re equal (though positional adjustment tries to capture that on average). - Fit: roster construction, payroll, and alternatives. So WAR is a strong summary, but you can look at component breakdown to understand “how” the value is produced and how stable it might be.
Can you explain why WAR is linear? Baseball wins aren’t linear in runs because of Pythagorean expectation.

Good point. The relationship between run differential and wins is nonlinear across the full range, but around typical team performance, it’s approximately linear. WAR uses a linear runs-to-wins conversion (RPW) as a local approximation: - For a team near .500, adding 10 runs might add about 1 win. - For extremely good or bad teams, the marginal runs-to-wins slope changes slightly, but using a constant is a practical simplification. Also, WAR is an individual metric; applying nonlinear team-level win models to individual contributions gets complicated, especially because run contributions interact (distribution of runs matters, not just total). So linear conversion is a pragmatic approximation that works reasonably well for most comparisons.
Is WAR additive across players without interaction? What about two great defenders overlapping coverage?

WAR assumes additivity: each player’s runs above average can be summed. But real baseball has interactions: - Defensive positioning and coverage overlap: two great OF defenders can reduce each other’s opportunities, making individual defensive metrics tricky. - Pitcher/fielder synergy: groundball pitchers benefit more from good infield defense. - Lineup interactions: batting order affects run distribution, though context-neutral batting runs ignore this. WAR is an accounting model, not a full simulation. It’s useful and mostly additive, but not perfectly interaction-free.
I want to understand how team defense is allocated to pitchers in bWAR. You said it’s based on balls in play. Can you be more explicit?

At a high level, bWAR’s goal is to separate: - Pitcher contribution to runs allowed from - Team defense contribution A plausible allocation approach (conceptually): 1) Estimate team defensive runs saved (from DRS-like system) relative to average. 2) Convert that to a per-ball-in-play or per-inning rate. 3) Allocate to individual pitchers based on their share of balls in play allowed (or innings pitched), possibly adjusting for strikeouts and home runs since those don’t involve fielders. 4) Adjust pitcher’s runs allowed baseline accordingly: - If defense saved runs behind him, reduce pitcher credit (since some run prevention is defense). - If defense cost runs, increase pitcher credit (since his RA9 was harmed by defense). The exact implementation details are proprietary-ish or at least complex, but that’s the conceptual structure.
That makes sense. Now, for fWAR, since it’s FIP-based, does it ignore batted ball types entirely? That seems like a limitation.

Standard FIP ignores batted ball types beyond HR, yes. That’s a limitation if you believe pitchers have repeatable skill in: - inducing ground balls vs fly balls, - suppressing hard contact, - limiting extra-base hits on balls in play, - generating pop-ups. Empirically: - Some of these are skills (GB rate is somewhat repeatable). - Others are noisier (BABIP suppression is less repeatable but not zero). That’s why people use: - xFIP (adds FB and normalizes HR/FB), - SIERA (adds GB and interactions), - Statcast-based expected metrics. But FanGraphs WAR historically chose FIP because it’s simple, stable, and captures major pitcher-controlled outcomes. It’s a modeling choice, not a claim that balls in play never matter.
So if a pitcher consistently beats his FIP via weak contact, fWAR will underrate him?

Relative to RA9-based measures, yes, in that scenario. A pitcher who: - allows fewer hits on balls in play than average due to weak contact, - suppresses doubles/triples, - strands runners at higher rates for skill-based reasons, could have ERA/RA9 better than FIP. fWAR would not fully capture that if it’s not reflected in HR/BB/K. bWAR (RA9-based) would credit the actual run prevention more (after defense adjustment), though it might still debate how much is pitcher vs defense/catcher/park.
Conversely, if a pitcher has great K/BB/HR but gives up lots of hits on balls in play, fWAR might overrate him?

Yes. If the poor results on balls in play are due to: - bad luck, - poor defense, - sequencing, fWAR might be closer to “true” skill and bWAR might underrate him. But if it’s due to: - consistently hard contact, - poor ability to manage contact, then fWAR might overrate him relative to actual value. This is the fundamental tradeoff: descriptive vs component-based evaluation.
If I’m building a model for player valuation, should I use WAR at all, or should I rebuild from components?

Many analysts use WAR as a starting point because it provides: - a common currency (wins), - a well-known baseline (replacement), - readily available components. But for valuation/forecasting, it’s often better to: - use component stats (batting runs, baserunning, defense, K/BB/HR, Statcast), - apply your own regression/aging curves, - model uncertainty and playing time. WAR is great as a summary and as a historical target variable, but if you’re making forward-looking decisions, decomposing is usually better.

I want to check if I understand the math. For hitters, WAR is basically: (wRAA + BsR + Fielding + Pos + Rep + Lg) / RPW. For pitchers, fWAR is: ( (lgFIP - FIP) * IP/9 + RepRuns ) / RPW. Is that accurate?

Yes, that’s a good simplified representation. For hitters: WAR ≈ (BattingRunsAA + BaserunningRuns + FieldingRuns + PosRuns + LeagueAdj + RepRuns) / RPW For pitchers (fWAR): WAR ≈ ( (lgFIP - pFIP) * IP/9 + RepRuns ) / RPW With the note that: - Some implementations include small additional adjustments (role, leverage, etc.). - The exact constants and definitions (PA, IP, park factors) matter.

What about the league average in wRAA: is it league-wide or position-specific? You said league-wide. So a catcher with wOBA equal to league average gets 0 wRAA, then plus catcher positional adjustment?

Correct. wRAA compares to overall league-average hitting. Then catcher gets positive positional runs. So an average-hitting catcher is more valuable than an average-hitting first baseman, all else equal, because catcher defense/position scarcity is higher.
How do you treat pinch hitters and defensive replacements? Their playing time is skewed.

WAR still works mechanically: - Pinch hitters: batting runs scale with PA; replacement runs also scale with PA, so their WAR per PA can be high if they hit well. But they may have little/no fielding innings, and if they’re a DH-like player, positional adjustment might be applied based on the minimal fielding time or a default position classification, which can vary by provider. - Defensive replacements: they might get few PA but many defensive innings. They can accumulate positive WAR via defense and positional adjustment even with negative batting runs, but small samples make it volatile. Providers have rules about assigning a “primary position” for positional adjustment if innings are low; that can affect bench players.
Does WAR account for “replacement” being different for hitters and pitchers? Like the distribution of talent isn’t symmetric.

Yes, implicitly through separate replacement run constants for batting and pitching. But the overall replacement concept is consistent: a baseline level of performance you can acquire cheaply. Because pitching and hitting are different labor markets, the replacement level in runs per PA is not directly comparable to replacement level in runs per IP; each is calibrated within its domain so that the overall league WAR aligns with the replacement win baseline.
Is there any circularity in setting replacement level so that total WAR matches league wins above replacement?

There is a calibration element, but it’s not purely circular; it’s choosing a baseline definition. Replacement level is a modeling choice. Once you choose it (e.g., .294 team win%), the implied total WAR in the league is determined. Then component scaling is adjusted so that the accounting works. You could choose a different replacement baseline (say .320), and then: - average full-time players would have less WAR, - total league WAR would be smaller. The relative ranking of players wouldn’t change much, but the absolute scale would. So WAR’s absolute scale is anchored by a convention, but within that scale the component calculations are grounded in run values.
How does WAR relate to salary? People say $/WAR. Is that valid?

It’s a common approximation: - Estimate how many dollars teams pay per marginal win on the free agent market. - Multiply a player’s projected WAR by $/WAR to estimate market value. - Compare to salary for surplus value. Caveats: - $/WAR varies by year, market conditions, CBA rules, revenue. - WAR itself has uncertainty. - Not all WAR is equally priced: scarcity, postseason value, and risk matter. - Pre-arb and arbitration players are underpaid relative to market; free agents are closer to market. But as a benchmarking tool, $/WAR is widely used.
Could you explain why $/WAR tends to rise over time?

Drivers: - Revenue growth (TV deals, streaming, sponsorship). - Inflation in player salaries. - Competitive dynamics: teams bidding for wins. - Changes in roster constraints and luxury tax thresholds. Because WAR is on a roughly stable win scale, as dollars in the system increase, $/WAR tends to increase.
Let’s go back: If I want to compute WAR myself for a hitter, what data do I need?

Minimum data for a simplified FanGraphs-like hitter WAR: Offense: - Player wOBA (or event counts to compute it) - League wOBA - wOBA scale - PA - Park factor (to adjust batting runs if you want) Baserunning: - BsR (or the underlying baserunning events: SB, CS, advancement on singles/doubles/flies, outs on bases, etc.) Defense: - A fielding runs metric (UZR/DRS/OAA converted to runs) - Innings by position Positional adjustment: - Positional constants - Innings by position Replacement runs: - Replacement runs per PA constant - PA League adjustment: - If used, a per-PA or per-player constant Runs per win: - Seasonal RPW constant Then: RAR = BatRuns + BaseRuns + FieldRuns + PosRuns + LgAdj + RepRuns WAR = RAR / RPW If you want “official” alignment with a provider, you must match their exact definitions and constants.
And for pitchers?

For fWAR-style pitchers: - HR, BB (minus IBB if excluding), HBP, K - IP - League FIP constant and league FIP - Park factor (especially HR park factor) - Replacement level constant for pitchers (per 9) - Runs per win Compute: - FIP - RAA = (lgFIP - pFIP) * IP/9 - Rrep = (replacement_gap_per9) * IP/9 - RAR = RAA + Rrep - WAR = RAR / RPW For bWAR-style pitchers: - Runs allowed (R) and/or RA9, IP - Park factor and league adjustments - Defense adjustment estimate - Replacement runs - Runs per win
One more nuance: how does WAR treat strike-shortened seasons? Is replacement per PA the same?

Replacement per PA is generally the same rate, so total replacement runs scale with playing time. In a shortened season: - A full-time player has fewer PA, so they accumulate fewer replacement runs and fewer batting runs. - WAR totals across the league are lower because there are fewer games/wins in the season. So WAR remains consistent per opportunity; it just sums to a smaller total because the season is shorter.
I think I’ve got the basics. Now, debate time: which is “better,” fWAR or bWAR?

“Better” depends on the question. If you want: - A measure tied to actual runs allowed/scored outcomes (descriptive value): bWAR leans that way, especially for pitchers. - A measure based on defense-independent pitching components (predictive-ish): fWAR for pitchers. - For position players: differences are mostly defense metric choice; neither is universally better. DRS and UZR each have strengths/weaknesses. Many analysts look at both: - If they agree, confidence increases. - If they diverge, examine why (defense, pitching estimator, park factors) and use additional evidence.
Suppose I’m doing awards analysis (MVP/Cy Young). Should I use bWAR because it reflects what happened?

For awards (a retrospective question), many prefer bWAR for pitchers because it aligns with actual runs allowed (after adjustments). But: - Some prefer fWAR because it credits underlying pitching performance independent of defense/luck, which can also be seen as “value.” - For hitters, either WAR is fine; defensive differences can matter, so looking at both is helpful. Ultimately, awards are subjective. WAR is a strong summary, but voters may consider narrative, playing time, team context, and other metrics.
Can you outline a process to diagnose a pitcher with big fWAR vs bWAR discrepancy?

Yes. Checklist: 1) Compare ERA, RA9, and FIP: - If ERA << FIP: pitcher “outperformed” FIP (sequencing, contact, defense, park). - If ERA >> FIP: “underperformed” FIP. 2) Look at BABIP and HR/FB: - Low BABIP and low HR/FB can drive ERA below FIP (though HR is in FIP; HR/FB matters if HR totals are low). 3) Strand rate (LOB%): - High LOB% can suppress ERA relative to FIP. 4) Defense and catcher: - Team defense quality; catcher framing (can affect K/BB and called strikes). 5) Park: - Some parks suppress hits/HR in ways not fully captured. 6) Contact quality (Statcast): - Hard-hit rate, barrel rate, xwOBA allowed, xERA. If contact quality is excellent, beating FIP may be more skill-based. 7) Role: - Reliever leverage and inherited runners can distort perceptions. This can explain most discrepancies.
Do the same for hitters with fWAR vs bWAR discrepancies.

For hitters: 1) Defense metric difference: - Compare UZR vs DRS (or OAA). If one says +15 and the other says -5, that’s the discrepancy. 2) Position/innings classification: - Check how many innings at each position on each site. Positional runs might differ if one classifies more innings at SS vs 2B. 3) Baserunning: - Compare baserunning runs components; some sites include GIDP differently. 4) Park factor: - Compare park-adjusted batting runs; especially for players traded midseason or in extreme parks. 5) League adjustments: - Usually small, but check for historical DH differences. Usually you’ll find defense is 80% of the story.
Let’s close with a compact summary I can keep in mind: what is WAR, how is it computed, and why fWAR and bWAR differ?

WAR is an all-in-one estimate of player value in wins versus replacement. Compute it by translating batting, baserunning, and fielding (plus positional and replacement adjustments) into runs above replacement, then dividing by runs-per-win. For pitchers, do the same using either a component estimator (FIP for fWAR) or runs allowed (RA9 for bWAR) with context and defense adjustments. fWAR and bWAR differ mainly because: - they use different defensive metrics for position players (UZR vs DRS), - they use different pitching foundations (FIP vs RA9), - and they apply slightly different park factors, replacement levels, and conversion constants. If you want, we can pick an actual player-season and walk through the component tables to see precisely where the gap comes from.
When and why did teams stop using a flat 4-4-2? What tactical problem was the 4-3-3 solving?

The “flat” 4-4-2 never truly disappeared, but it stopped being the default at the top level as the game’s pressing schemes, central overloads, and buildup structures evolved. The short version is: a strict two-man central midfield in a flat 4-4-2 became increasingly vulnerable to being outnumbered and out-positioned in the middle, especially against teams using a 4-3-3 (or 4-2-3-1/4-1-4-1) that could create a spare player either in the first phase (build-up) or in midfield. A few key “problems” the 4-3-3 was solving: 1) Central superiority and better spacing - In a traditional 4-4-2, the two central midfielders have to cover huge lateral and vertical distances. Against a 4-3-3, they’re often facing three central mids (or two plus a #10 dropping), which creates constant dilemmas: step out to press and open passing lanes behind, or hold and concede progression. - The 4-3-3 allows a single pivot (#6) plus two interiors (#8s) to occupy different vertical lanes: one can press, one can cover, one can provide a passing outlet. 2) Pressing and counterpressing organization - As pressing became more coordinated, teams wanted clearer reference points for triggers and cover shadows. A 4-3-3 can press in a 4-1-4-1 shape, with the winger pressing the fullback, the striker splitting center-backs, and the far winger tucking in to protect the pivot lane. - In a flat 4-4-2, pressing can be strong (especially with a narrow block), but it can be harder to prevent passes into a single pivot if the two strikers get split or if the midfield line gets pinned. 3) Build-up and the “extra man” concept - Many 4-3-3 teams build with a 3-2 structure (fullback inverts or a center-back steps in), giving stability against a two-striker press. - The 4-4-2 can build well too, but the classic version often relies on direct play into two forwards and second balls, rather than consistent positional play. Historically, you can trace the shift through phases: - Late 80s/90s: variations of 4-4-2 still dominant, but teams begin adding a “third midfielder” either via a diamond (4-4-2 diamond) or a withdrawn forward (4-4-1-1). - Early/mid 2000s: 4-2-3-1 becomes popular (essentially 4-4-2 with one striker dropped and more central control). - Late 2000s/2010s: 4-3-3 and hybrid structures become common, especially with pressing and positional play. If you want, we can pin this down by era and examples: which leagues/teams you’re thinking of (Premier League, Serie A, international tournaments), and whether you mean “flat 4-4-2 mid-block” or “two banks of four” as a defensive phase.
